Alex Martinez: Suggest listing language:
- “Hosted supports region selection. Data is processed in-region by default. Optional multi-region failover can be enabled.”
Then security doc has the full “what is in-region vs global metadata.”

Marcus Lin: Summarizing edits we’ll apply:
- Remove AES‑256 mention; keep “encrypted at rest” + “TLS 1.2+”
- Training: add “without explicit permission/opt-in”
- Retention: remove “30 days payload” claim; split metadata vs content; move concrete defaults to security packet
- Audit logs: “available in console + API; export options plan-dependent; event types listed; not ‘all events’”
- KMS/CMK: qualify to Hosted vs Dedicated/Private + “select systems”
- Residency: “processed in-region by default; note control-plane metadata + optional failover”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Approved direction. Please also add “shared responsibility” sentence somewhere (marketplace reviewers expect it). Something like “Redwood secures the platform; customers are responsible for securing their accounts, API keys, and client-side usage.”

Olga Petrov: And ensure the security overview references SOC 2 Type II coverage without implying certification scope beyond what we have. Use “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA/on request.”

Elliot Price: I’ll update the docs PR copy accordingly and tag @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for final pass. Also will add a “Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private” table so we stop mixing claims.

Alex Martinez: Once wording is updated, please paste the final “approved marketplace-safe copy block” back here so PMM can lift it directly.

Marcus Lin: Will do. I’ll post the revised block later today after I integrate Priya’s notes on retention + residency.

Priya Natarajan: If helpful I can drop exact current defaults for (a) payload sampling retention (b) metrics retention (c) audit log retention per plan. But agree—keep it in packet, not listing.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: Also please sanity check that “TLS 1.2+” matches our edge config everywhere (incl. older SDKs). If any endpoints are still negotiating 1.2 only, still fine, but don’t claim 1.3.

Marcus Lin: Ack. I’ll verify with edge config and keep it “TLS 1.2+” only.

Daniel Carter: When you update the claim block, please ensure we link to the evidence index page, not directly to internal Jira/Confluence. Marketplace reviewers will need something shareable.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Thanks all. (reaction: :white_check_mark: x4)
text:
Marcus Lin: Dropping proposed marketplace security claims here for final accuracy check before Docs publishes + PMM puts into AWS/GCP listing. Please nitpick wording.

Claims (draft):
1) “All customer data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES‑256).”
2) “Customer content (prompts/outputs) is not used to train models.”
3) “Hosted API request/response payloads are retained for 30 days by default for abuse monitoring and debugging; customers can request shorter retention.”
4) “Audit logs are available for admin actions and API key events, and can be exported to customer SIEM.”
5) “Keys are managed via cloud KMS; Dedicated/Private customers can use customer-managed keys.”
6) “Data residency: processing occurs in the region selected by the customer (where available).”

Need to confirm what’s true across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, and what needs qualifiers.

Naomi Feldman: :eyes: going through one by one. Overall ok but some are over-broad. We need product-mode qualifiers or AWS/GCP reviewers will bounce it.

Naomi Feldman: 1) “AES‑256 at rest” is usually fine but we should say “encrypted at rest using industry-standard encryption” unless we can prove every storage layer is AES‑256 (object store, DB, backups, logs). Also “TLS 1.2+” ok, but note some internal service-to-service is mTLS—don’t overspec.

Olga Petrov: +1. From compliance standpoint, over-specific crypto claims create evidence burden. Better: “encrypted at rest and in transit” and keep TLS 1.2+ if we have a standard.

Marcus Lin: Got it. We can change to “TLS 1.2+” + “encryption at rest” without naming AES. Or “AES‑256 where supported by underlying cloud services”?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Don’t do “where supported” — reads weak. Prefer: “Data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest using industry-standard encryption. Encryption is provided by major cloud provider managed services for Hosted.”

Elliot Price: For docs, I can implement mode-specific callouts: Hosted / Dedicated / Private. Listing copy is tighter though. We can keep listing generic and link to full security page.

Alex Martinez: +1, marketplace pages can be brief and point to the “Marketplace Security Overview” doc. But the claims on listing still need to be defensible.

Marcus Lin: 2) “not used to train models” — true for customer content? I know we don’t train foundation models, but do we use any prompts/outputs for eval/quality monitoring if customer opts in?

Naomi Feldman: We have an opt-in program for sharing data for quality improvements (explicit). Default is no. Wording should be: “We do not use customer content to train Redwood or third-party foundation models without explicit customer permission.”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Yes. Avoid absolute “never” statements. Add “without explicit permission” / “unless customer opts in.”

Daniel Carter: From evidence POV, we can point to policy + DPA language. But the marketing claim should match the policy exactly.

Marcus Lin: 3) Retention: “30 days by default” — is that accurate for Hosted logs? I recall payload retention is shorter in prod unless debug is enabled.

Priya Natarajan: Looping in (platform). For Hosted: request metadata (timings, token counts, error codes) retained longer for billing/analytics. Full payload retention is *not* 30 days universally. We store payloads only for a subset of traffic for abuse detection + customer support debugging, and it’s configurable per tenant. Default is currently 7 days for payload sampling, 30 days for derived logs/metrics. (reaction: :warning: x3)

Naomi Feldman: This is exactly why we need to split “payload” vs “metadata.” Marketplace reviewers also ask specifically about “customer content.” Payload == customer content.

Marcus Lin: Ok so claim 3 as written is wrong. Proposed rewrite:
- “By default, Redwood retains limited request metadata for billing and operational monitoring. Customer content (prompt/response payloads) is not retained except as necessary for abuse prevention and support, and retention can be configured by plan.”

Alex Martinez: That’s closer. We should also provide a concrete number somewhere in the security doc, but maybe not in the listing. Buyers ask “how many days” though.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Put the exact defaults in the security packet (versioned) and keep listing language high-level. If defaults change, we don’t want to update marketplace listings every time.

Olga Petrov: +1. Also, ensure we mention “deletion within X days of retention expiration” if we have that in policy.

Marcus Lin: 4) Audit logs: We definitely log admin actions + API key events in console. Do we have guaranteed export to SIEM today or is it “available via API” and customer builds integration?

Priya Natarajan: Audit log export: currently available via API + S3 sink for Dedicated (feature flag). Hosted has API access; SIEM integrations are “customer managed” (Splunk, etc.) via webhook/collector on their side. No turnkey “one-click Splunk app.”

Elliot Price: So the claim should be: “Audit logs can be accessed via the Redwood Console and API; export options available (plan-dependent).”

Naomi Feldman: Yes, and list events: “org membership changes, role changes, API key create/revoke, policy changes, model deployment/rollout actions.” Don’t claim “all events.”

Marcus Lin: 5) KMS + CMK: “Keys are managed via cloud KMS; Dedicated/Private can use customer-managed keys.” True for Dedicated? I think we support CMK for certain storage only.

Priya Natarajan: Dedicated: CMK supported for object storage + log storage when deployed in customer VPC / dedicated environment, not for every internal service. Private (customer-controlled) can integrate with customer KMS/HSM depending on deployment.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Wording suggestion:
- “Hosted: encryption keys are managed by Redwood using cloud-provider key management services.”
- “Dedicated/Private: customer-managed keys are supported for select storage systems; details available in security packet.”

Olga Petrov: “select storage systems” is safe. Also avoid “HSM” unless we can deliver it across clouds consistently.

Marcus Lin: 6) Data residency: “processing occurs in region selected” — but fallback routing could cross-region if customer enables it, right?

Naomi Feldman: Yes. Also Dedicated/Private are pinned, Hosted has region choice but some control-plane metadata might be global.

Priya Natarajan: Correct. In Hosted, control plane is multi-region but some auth/billing metadata replicated. We do not move payload cross-region unless customer has multi-region failover enabled. Need that nuance.

Alex Martinez: Suggest listing language:
- “Hosted supports region selection. Data is processed in-region by default. Optional multi-region failover can be enabled.”
Then security doc has the full “what is in-region vs global metadata.”

Marcus Lin: Summarizing edits we’ll apply:
- Remove AES‑256 mention; keep “encrypted at rest” + “TLS 1.2+”
- Training: add “without explicit permission/opt-in”
- Retention: remove “30 days payload” claim; split metadata vs content; move concrete defaults to security packet
- Audit logs: “available in console + API; export options plan-dependent; event types listed; not ‘all events’”
- KMS/CMK: qualify to Hosted vs Dedicated/Private + “select systems”
- Residency: “processed in-region by default; note control-plane metadata + optional failover”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Approved direction. Please also add “shared responsibility” sentence somewhere (marketplace reviewers expect it). Something like “Redwood secures the platform; customers are responsible for securing their accounts, API keys, and client-side usage.”

Olga Petrov: And ensure the security overview references SOC 2 Type II coverage without implying certification scope beyond what we have. Use “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA/on request.”

Elliot Price: I’ll update the docs PR copy accordingly and tag @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for final pass. Also will add a “Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private” table so we stop mixing claims.

Alex Martinez: Once wording is updated, please paste the final “approved marketplace-safe copy block” back here so PMM can lift it directly.

Marcus Lin: Will do. I’ll post the revised block later today after I integrate Priya’s notes on retention + residency.

Priya Natarajan: If helpful I can drop exact current defaults for (a) payload sampling retention (b) metrics retention (c) audit log retention per plan. But agree—keep it in packet, not listing.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: Also please sanity check that “TLS 1.2+” matches our edge config everywhere (incl. older SDKs). If any endpoints are still negotiating 1.2 only, still fine, but don’t claim 1.3.

Marcus Lin: Ack. I’ll verify with edge config and keep it “TLS 1.2+” only.

Daniel Carter: When you update the claim block, please ensure we link to the evidence index page, not directly to internal Jira/Confluence. Marketplace reviewers will need something shareable.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Thanks all. (reaction: :white_check_mark: x4)

source.thread:
Marcus Lin: quick wording review needed on retention/deletion language before we sign off on the FAQ + one-pager updates. this is the current draft block (shows up in a few places):

"Redwood does not store customer data. Requests are processed in-memory and deleted immediately after completion. Logs contain no customer content. Customers can request deletion at any time."

this feels… too absolute. can folks (esp @Olga Petrov / @Daniel Carter) mark up what we should NOT say + suggested replacements? relates to PM-4860 + docs PR-2860.

Olga Petrov: :eyes: yeah we need to remove a few absolutes here.
- “does not store customer data” is ambiguous (someone will read as *no persistence anywhere* + *no backups* + *no telemetry*).
- “deleted immediately” is a red flag in questionnaires. unless we can defend “immediately” with evidence + definition.
- “logs contain no customer content” is also too broad. we have multiple log types; some can include request/response depending on config/support tooling.

Suggestion: separate (1) customer content (prompts/outputs/files) (2) operational metadata/telemetry (3) audit logs.

Daniel Carter: +1. also watch “Customers can request deletion at any time” — we can say we support deletion requests / DSR process, but “at any time” reads like instant, self-serve, and applies to all systems including security logs (usually not true).

Kevin Osei: agree. we need to define what we mean by “customer data”. for a lot of customers the question is: do you retain prompt/response content, and if so how long + can we configure it.

for Hosted, we do have some short-lived retention for service operation (debugging/abuse detection) depending on plan + settings. we should avoid implying “never retained”.

Hanae Suzuki: also the “processed in-memory” line is risky. technically requests traverse services and may hit buffers/queues; also crash dumps / tracing can capture fragments if not configured right. we’ve got controls but saying “in-memory only” isn’t something i’d want to stand behind universally.

Naomi Feldman: from Product Sec PM side: please don’t commit to “no customer content in logs” globally. the better statement is:
- default behavior by deployment mode
- configurable controls / opt-outs where available
- we don’t use customer content to train our models (separate claim)

and we should link to the customer-facing retention overview as the canonical source.

Elliot Price: can we align this with the confluence doc we’re about to publish? i’m looking at the latest draft for the customer-facing overview and it already avoids “immediate” language. would be good if the FAQ snippet mirrors it so we don’t end up with contradictions.

Kira Thompson: +1. also in docs PR-2860 we added a glossary section (“Customer Content”, “Service Metadata”, “Audit Events”). we should use those terms verbatim.

Marcus Lin: helpful. can someone propose replacement copy block we can reuse in FAQ + RFP library? ideally 4-6 sentences, low drama, no numbers unless we’re sure.

Olga Petrov: draft proposal (GRC-friendly):

"Redwood processes Customer Content (e.g., prompts and outputs) to provide the service. Depending on the deployment mode and configuration, Redwood may temporarily retain limited Customer Content and associated metadata for service operation, troubleshooting, abuse prevention, and security monitoring. Retention periods vary by product and customer settings; Redwood provides options to reduce or disable retention of certain content logs for eligible plans and deployments. Redwood maintains audit and security logs for access and administrative events; these logs are separate from Customer Content and may be retained for security and compliance purposes. Customers may request deletion of Customer Content in accordance with contractual terms and applicable law."

needs a tech sanity check.

Kevin Osei: mostly good. i’d tweak “may temporarily retain limited Customer Content” to explicitly say “when enabled” or “in certain configurations” so it doesn’t sound like we always retain content.

also add “Redwood does not use Customer Content to train foundation models” somewhere, but maybe that’s a separate Q/A.

Hanae Suzuki: i’d also avoid “troubleshooting” without guardrails; it implies humans reading prompts/outputs. if we keep it, specify “authorized personnel” + least privilege + access logging.

Dev Patel: from Support side: a ton of tickets come from the phrase “we don’t store your data” and then the customer asks “then what are these logs?”

what works better is: “we can minimize/disable content retention” + “audit logs are separate”. and explicitly say “support access is controlled and logged”.

Daniel Carter: +1 on human access guardrails. auditors/customers ask: who can access content logs, and is access logged.

maybe add: “Access to any retained content is restricted to authorized personnel and is logged.”

Elliot Price: for docs consistency, we should keep “Customer Content” and “Service Metadata” as defined terms, and avoid “customer data” entirely in this block.

Kira Thompson: wording tweak suggestion (keeping it short):

"Redwood processes Customer Content (such as prompts and outputs) to provide the service. By default and depending on deployment mode and configuration, Redwood may retain limited Customer Content and Service Metadata for a limited period to operate and secure the service (e.g., troubleshooting, abuse prevention, and incident investigation). Customers may have options to reduce or disable retention of certain content logs for eligible plans and deployments. Redwood also maintains audit and security logs for administrative and access events; these are separate from Customer Content and may be retained for compliance and security purposes. Access to any retained content is restricted to authorized personnel and is logged."

Kevin Osei: ^ looks good. i’d remove “By default” unless we want to imply default retention is on. could be read wrong. maybe “Depending on deployment mode and configuration” is enough.

Naomi Feldman: agree, drop “By default”. also add “limited period” twice feels redundant; keep one.

Marcus Lin: ack. revised based on comments:

"Redwood processes Customer Content (such as prompts and outputs) to provide the service. Depending on deployment mode and configuration, Redwood may retain limited Customer Content and Service Metadata for a limited period to operate and secure the service (e.g., troubleshooting, abuse prevention, and incident investigation). Customers may have options to reduce or disable retention of certain content logs for eligible plans and deployments. Redwood also maintains audit and security logs for administrative and access events; these are separate from Customer Content and may be retained for compliance and security purposes. Access to any retained content is restricted to authorized personnel and is logged."

GRC ok with this?

Olga Petrov: GRC ok :thumbsup: 

but add one note for the kit: avoid answering “what is the exact number of days” unless you’re pulling from the retention overview page (and if region-specific). we should keep the FAQ high-level + point to canonical doc.

Daniel Carter: +1. also in RFPs, when asked “immediate deletion,” use “in accordance with contractual terms / configured retention / legal holds may apply” language. don’t promise impossible timelines.

Hanae Suzuki: Security Eng ok. one more nuance: “incident investigation” is fine but make sure we’re not implying customer content is always required; still ok.

Elliot Price: i’ll mirror this exact block into the docs PR where we reference the FAQ language (so it stays consistent). can someone drop the link to the retention overview page once it’s updated?

Marcus Lin: will do. i’ll update the FAQ + claims matrix with this block and flag the anti-claims:
- don’t say “no storage”
- don’t say “in-memory only”
- don’t say “deleted immediately”

closing the loop w/ @Naomi Feldman once the PRs land.

Kira Thompson: :white_check_mark: i’ll also add a short “do not use” callout in the docs author notes so we stop reintroducing “immediately”.

Dev Patel: thank you. this will save us a bunch of back-and-forth in support threads.

Marcus Lin: last ask: if anyone sees “logs contain no customer content” in other snippets, please tag me. i found it in one older RFP block too.

Olga Petrov: i’ll scan the RFP library draft for those phrases and open a quick cleanup list.

source.body:
Hanae Suzuki: heads up: getting more field questions on audit log exports (esp. Aurora + Summit). can we sanity check what we *actually* support today + what fields we include? they’re asking for “SIEM integration”, “near real-time”, “JSON schema”, “who/what/when” etc.

Markus Klein: +1. had 2 SE calls this week where security team asked for sample payload + whether it includes source IP + API key / user identity + request/response bodies.

Fatima Noor: also from a regulated prospect: they want to know if export works for Dedicated + Private (VPC) and if logs can stay in-region (EU).

Marcus Lin: ok, current state (pls correct if i miss anything):
- Audit logs exist for Console + API actions (auth, key mgmt, org/user/admin actions, model deployment changes, policy changes, export config changes)
- We DO NOT log prompt/response content in audit logs (that’s app telemetry / request logs, separate)
- Export: supported destination is customer-owned S3-compatible bucket (AWS S3 + S3-compatible endpoints). Delivery is batched (not guaranteed real-time). Typical delay: minutes.
- Format: JSON lines (one event per line). Schema is stable-ish but we’ve added fields; need docs to reflect.

Kevin Osei: yep. also worth calling out: we can generate export into the same region as the control plane / deployment, but “EU residency” depends on customer deployment mode + region selection. don’t want AEs saying “we guarantee EU-only for everything” unless it’s actually configured.

Markus Klein: what about Splunk? some questionnaires ask “native Splunk integration” or “webhook”.

Marcus Lin: no “native” Splunk app today. we can tell them: S3 export + customer can ingest to Splunk (or other SIEM) via their pipeline. anything else needs to be framed as “not currently supported” / “contact SE for options” (no roadmap promises).

Naomi Feldman: +1 please be careful with “near-real-time SIEM” wording. if they need sub-minute streaming, that’s not something we should imply. i’ve seen draft language trending too strong.

Hanae Suzuki: can we list the fields we include so SEs can answer questionnaires without guessing?

Kevin Osei: event fields (high level) we have in the payload today:
- event_id
- event_time (RFC3339)
- actor_type (user/service)
- actor_id (internal)
- actor_email (if user)
- actor_ip (when available; for some internal-to-service paths it’s empty)
- user_agent (console events)
- org_id / project_id
- action (string enum)
- resource_type + resource_id
- result (success/failure)
- error_code (if failure)
- metadata (limited key/value; e.g. model_id, region) — note this changes by event type

Marcus Lin: + also: we intentionally do NOT include secrets (API key material), and we do NOT include full request/response payloads. For “who did what” it’s actor + action + resource + time + result.

Markus Klein: got it. question from Aurora: “does it cover token usage / billing events?”

Marcus Lin: no, that’s usage analytics, not audit logs. audit logs are security/admin events. we can say: usage/metrics available in Console + API, but it’s separate from audit log stream.

Fatima Noor: in Private deployments, can audit logs be exported to customer’s SIEM directly inside their VPC?

Kevin Osei: for Private: depends on the package. baseline: audit logs still exist, export is configurable (S3-compatible). if they have strict egress controls, we need to make sure the export destination is reachable (usually customer-owned endpoint). if they want “no outbound”, they can point to an internal S3-compatible store. but we should say “S3-compatible” not “any SIEM”.

Naomi Feldman: and we should align on the exact phrasing for Private: “customer-managed networking; exports to customer-controlled destination” but don’t imply unlimited destinations.

Elliot Price: docs gap: current public-ish doc only says “audit logs + export available” but doesn’t list event taxonomy/fields or delivery semantics.

Kira Thompson: i can update the docs page to include:
- event categories (auth/access, admin/config, org/user mgmt)
- sample JSONL line
- export destination (S3-compatible) + batching/latency statement
- what’s *not* included (prompt/response bodies, secrets)
If we have a canonical internal schema doc, pls link.

Marcus Lin: we have an internal snippet in the Confluence “Audit logging customer overview” draft, but it’s light. i’ll drop a sample event here (redacted) so you can reuse:
```json
{"event_id":"01J2X...","event_time":"2026-01-21T19:12:44Z","org_id":"org_...","project_id":"proj_...","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"usr_...","actor_email":"admin@customer.com","actor_ip":"203.0.113.42","user_agent":"Mozilla/5.0...","action":"api_key.created","resource_type":"api_key","resource_id":"key_...","result":"success","metadata":{"scope":"project","key_name":"prod-ci"}}
```

Hanae Suzuki: :thumbsup: that sample will help a ton. also can we add a one-liner for questionnaires: “Exports available via S3, JSONL, batched delivery; integrates with SIEM via customer pipeline.”

Naomi Feldman: yes, but add “when available” for IP + UA, and “delivery latency not guaranteed” (don’t let it become an SLO).

Markus Klein: what do we say on retention? they ask “how long do you retain audit logs” + “can customer configure retention”.

Kevin Osei: retention depends on plan / deployment mode. baseline retention is finite; extended retention is an add-on. exports to customer bucket allow them to retain per their policy. do not quote exact days in Slack unless we confirm the current packaged numbers.

Marcus Lin: +1. say “audit logs retained for a configurable period depending on plan; customers can export to their own storage for longer-term retention.” Point them to the security kit retention one-pager once it’s refreshed.

Elliot Price: action items:
1) @Kira Thompson draft doc updates (fields + sample + delivery semantics + limitations)
2) @Marcus Lin / @Kevin Osei review for accuracy + “do-not-say” compliance
3) @Naomi Feldman sanity check wording (no roadmap / no SLO claims)
I’ll connect it to ENG-13988 / docs PR so we can ship.

Hanae Suzuki: i’ll reply back to SEs with the approved bullets once Kira has draft text. for now i’ll tell them: S3 export + JSONL + batched + no prompt/response in audit logs.

Markus Klein: thanks all. will also ping Sales that “Splunk native integration” = customer pipeline from S3 export.

Naomi Feldman: pls also note: if a prospect asks for a “complete log of every inference request”, that’s *not* audit logs. route them to observability/request logging story, but don’t conflate.

Kevin Osei: :100: + if they need evidence, follow the SOC2/evidence request playbook (NDA etc). don’t send internal screenshots.

Elliot Price: ack. dropping reminder link for internal folks: confluence -> Security & Compliance -> Audit logging overview (draft). will update once the new doc is published.


Hanae Suzuki: :thumbsup: that sample will help a ton. also can we add a one-liner for questionnaires: “Exports available via S3, JSONL, batched delivery; integrates with SIEM via customer pipeline.”

Naomi Feldman: yes, but add “when available” for IP + UA, and “delivery latency not guaranteed” (don’t let it become an SLO).

Markus Klein: what do we say on retention? they ask “how long do you retain audit logs” + “can customer configure retention”.

Kevin Osei: retention depends on plan / deployment mode. baseline retention is finite; extended retention is an add-on. exports to customer bucket allow them to retain per their policy. do not quote exact days in Slack unless we confirm the current packaged numbers.

Marcus Lin: +1. say “audit logs retained for a configurable period depending on plan; customers can export to their own storage for longer-term retention.” Point them to the security kit retention one-pager once it’s refreshed.

Elliot Price: action items:
1) @Kira Thompson draft doc updates (fields + sample + delivery semantics + limitations)
2) @Marcus Lin / @Kevin Osei review for accuracy + “do-not-say” compliance
3) @Naomi Feldman sanity check wording (no roadmap / no SLO claims)
I’ll connect it to ENG-13988 / docs PR so we can ship.

Hanae Suzuki: i’ll reply back to SEs with the approved bullets once Kira has draft text. for now i’ll tell them: S3 export + JSONL + batched + no prompt/response in audit logs.

Markus Klein: thanks all. will also ping Sales that “Splunk native integration” = customer pipeline from S3 export.

Naomi Feldman: pls also note: if a prospect asks for a “complete log of every inference request”, that’s *not* audit logs. route them to observability/request logging story, but don’t conflate.

Kevin Osei: :100: + if they need evidence, follow the SOC2/evidence request playbook (NDA etc). don’t send internal screenshots.

Elliot Price: ack. dropping reminder link for internal folks: confluence -> Security & Compliance -> Audit logging overview (draft). will update once the new doc is published.
text:
Hanae Suzuki: heads up: getting more field questions on audit log exports (esp. Aurora + Summit). can we sanity check what we *actually* support today + what fields we include? they’re asking for “SIEM integration”, “near real-time”, “JSON schema”, “who/what/when” etc.

Markus Klein: +1. had 2 SE calls this week where security team asked for sample payload + whether it includes source IP + API key / user identity + request/response bodies.

Fatima Noor: also from a regulated prospect: they want to know if export works for Dedicated + Private (VPC) and if logs can stay in-region (EU).

Marcus Lin: ok, current state (pls correct if i miss anything):
- Audit logs exist for Console + API actions (auth, key mgmt, org/user/admin actions, model deployment changes, policy changes, export config changes)
- We DO NOT log prompt/response content in audit logs (that’s app telemetry / request logs, separate)
- Export: supported destination is customer-owned S3-compatible bucket (AWS S3 + S3-compatible endpoints). Delivery is batched (not guaranteed real-time). Typical delay: minutes.
- Format: JSON lines (one event per line). Schema is stable-ish but we’ve added fields; need docs to reflect.

Kevin Osei: yep. also worth calling out: we can generate export into the same region as the control plane / deployment, but “EU residency” depends on customer deployment mode + region selection. don’t want AEs saying “we guarantee EU-only for everything” unless it’s actually configured.

Markus Klein: what about Splunk? some questionnaires ask “native Splunk integration” or “webhook”.

Marcus Lin: no “native” Splunk app today. we can tell them: S3 export + customer can ingest to Splunk (or other SIEM) via their pipeline. anything else needs to be framed as “not currently supported” / “contact SE for options” (no roadmap promises).

Naomi Feldman: +1 please be careful with “near-real-time SIEM” wording. if they need sub-minute streaming, that’s not something we should imply. i’ve seen draft language trending too strong.

Hanae Suzuki: can we list the fields we include so SEs can answer questionnaires without guessing?

Kevin Osei: event fields (high level) we have in the payload today:
- event_id
- event_time (RFC3339)
- actor_type (user/service)
- actor_id (internal)
- actor_email (if user)
- actor_ip (when available; for some internal-to-service paths it’s empty)
- user_agent (console events)
- org_id / project_id
- action (string enum)
- resource_type + resource_id
- result (success/failure)
- error_code (if failure)
- metadata (limited key/value; e.g. model_id, region) — note this changes by event type

Marcus Lin: + also: we intentionally do NOT include secrets (API key material), and we do NOT include full request/response payloads. For “who did what” it’s actor + action + resource + time + result.

Markus Klein: got it. question from Aurora: “does it cover token usage / billing events?”

Marcus Lin: no, that’s usage analytics, not audit logs. audit logs are security/admin events. we can say: usage/metrics available in Console + API, but it’s separate from audit log stream.

Fatima Noor: in Private deployments, can audit logs be exported to customer’s SIEM directly inside their VPC?

Kevin Osei: for Private: depends on the package. baseline: audit logs still exist, export is configurable (S3-compatible). if they have strict egress controls, we need to make sure the export destination is reachable (usually customer-owned endpoint). if they want “no outbound”, they can point to an internal S3-compatible store. but we should say “S3-compatible” not “any SIEM”.

Naomi Feldman: and we should align on the exact phrasing for Private: “customer-managed networking; exports to customer-controlled destination” but don’t imply unlimited destinations.

Elliot Price: docs gap: current public-ish doc only says “audit logs + export available” but doesn’t list event taxonomy/fields or delivery semantics.

Kira Thompson: i can update the docs page to include:
- event categories (auth/access, admin/config, org/user mgmt)
- sample JSONL line
- export destination (S3-compatible) + batching/latency statement
- what’s *not* included (prompt/response bodies, secrets)
If we have a canonical internal schema doc, pls link.

Marcus Lin: we have an internal snippet in the Confluence “Audit logging customer overview” draft, but it’s light. i’ll drop a sample event here (redacted) so you can reuse:
```json
{"event_id":"01J2X...","event_time":"2026-01-21T19:12:44Z","org_id":"org_...","project_id":"proj_...","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"usr_...","actor_email":"admin@customer.com","actor_ip":"203.0.113.42","user_agent":"Mozilla/5.0...","action":"api_key.created","resource_type":"api_key","resource_id":"key_...","result":"success","metadata":{"scope":"project","key_name":"prod-ci"}}
```

Hanae Suzuki: :thumbsup: that sample will help a ton. also can we add a one-liner for questionnaires: “Exports available via S3, JSONL, batched delivery; integrates with SIEM via customer pipeline.”

Naomi Feldman: yes, but add “when available” for IP + UA, and “delivery latency not guaranteed” (don’t let it become an SLO).

Markus Klein: what do we say on retention? they ask “how long do you retain audit logs” + “can customer configure retention”.

Kevin Osei: retention depends on plan / deployment mode. baseline retention is finite; extended retention is an add-on. exports to customer bucket allow them to retain per their policy. do not quote exact days in Slack unless we confirm the current packaged numbers.

Marcus Lin: +1. say “audit logs retained for a configurable period depending on plan; customers can export to their own storage for longer-term retention.” Point them to the security kit retention one-pager once it’s refreshed.

Elliot Price: action items:
1) @Kira Thompson draft doc updates (fields + sample + delivery semantics + limitations)
2) @Marcus Lin / @Kevin Osei review for accuracy + “do-not-say” compliance
3) @Naomi Feldman sanity check wording (no roadmap / no SLO claims)
I’ll connect it to ENG-13988 / docs PR so we can ship.

Hanae Suzuki: i’ll reply back to SEs with the approved bullets once Kira has draft text. for now i’ll tell them: S3 export + JSONL + batched + no prompt/response in audit logs.

Markus Klein: thanks all. will also ping Sales that “Splunk native integration” = customer pipeline from S3 export.

Naomi Feldman: pls also note: if a prospect asks for a “complete log of every inference request”, that’s *not* audit logs. route them to observability/request logging story, but don’t conflate.

Kevin Osei: :100: + if they need evidence, follow the SOC2/evidence request playbook (NDA etc). don’t send internal screenshots.

Elliot Price: ack. dropping reminder link for internal folks: confluence -> Security & Compliance -> Audit logging overview (draft). will update once the new doc is published.

source.thread:
June Park: heads-up — NovaMed private deploy reported short-lived 403s after maintenance. Apps threw decrypt denied for ~6m. :grimacing:
Marc Rivera: timeline? what was in that maintenance window?
June Park: scheduled key rotation at 03:20 UTC and we also rolled certs for keystore-proxy at 03:21 (attempted to reduce long-lived tokens). First 403s at 03:22.
sec-bot: Alert: decrypt_403 spike tenant=novamed at 2026-02-25T03:22:10Z. Audit ref: rot-cert-20260225.
Oncall Ops: sanitized logs: `2026-02-25T03:22:09Z keystore-proxy ERROR tenant=novamed msg="decrypt denied" code=403 principal=cert:CN=ks-proxy-ephemeral` and `hsm-connector WARN principal_lookup failed: no mapping for cert CN`.
Ava Chen: ah — cert rotation changed the presented identity (CN SAN). KMS policy is bound to the old service principal id, not the ephemeral cert mapping. We lost the cert->principal mapping during the cutover.
Marc Rivera: mitigation? avoid rolling cert identity and key alias at same instant.
Oncall Ops: manual fix applied: created mapping entry `cert-principal:ks-proxy-ephemeral -> svc-novamed-ks` and granted decrypt on new key version. Apps began succeeding within 90s. Command used: `kmsctl map-cert --cn ks-proxy-ephemeral --principal svc-novamed-ks && kmsctl grant --key v20260225 --principal svc-novamed-ks --actions decrypt`.
Ava Chen: follow-ups: 1) add preflight that asserts cert principal mapping exists before alias flip, 2) disallow simultaneous cert identity changes + global key alias swap in same window, 3) prefer short-lived cert rotation with stable mapping layer (map by kube serviceaccount UID) instead of CN churn, 4) add alert: >2 decrypt 403/min per tenant during rotation window.
June Park: filing INF-7001 (cert-mapping-preflight) and INF-7002 (rotation-serialization). Drafting customer note: concise timeline + remediation + attestation.
sec-bot: Attachments: rotation job events, cert-issue logs, manual mapping audit entry. Reminder: add cert->principal mapping smoke test to private-deploy preflight checklist.

source.messages:
Samir Patel: FYI CI scan just flagged an AWS access key embedded in the build logs from last night's nightly-run. found it in the artifact cache output. attaching quick grep result.
security-bot: Detection: high-confidence secret match (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID pattern) in workflow run 2026-02-19T03:12Z. SHA: a1b2c3.
Jess Liu: ugh. which repo and job?
Samir Patel: repo: infra/packer-images, job: nightly-build-ami. log path: s3://redwood-ci-artifacts/nightly/2026-02-18/logs/packer.log.gz
Ben Carter: copying grep: ```zcat packer.log.gz | grep -n "AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}" || true``` returned one hit with masked context. looks like a credential output by an errant script that printed `aws configure` debug.
Maria Gomez: immediate actions we should do: 1) revoke the key, 2) rotate any KMS-encrypted assets that used it (if necessary), 3) search other artifacts and commits.
oncall-ryan: I'm oncall, I'll take containment. Can someone tell which IAM user/key id it is?
Samir Patel: key id in the log: AKIAEXAMPLE12345 (masked in log, full value in raw). appears to be associated with user: ci-service-bot (arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/ci-service-bot)
Ben Carter: I'm running account-wide find. command I'm using: ```aws iam list-access-keys --user-name ci-service-bot --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[]'``` and a quick `aws sts get-caller-identity` check from the infra account.
Jess Liu: also run a repo history search to see if the key was ever committed: ```git rev-list --all | xargs -n1 git grep -I --line-number "AKIA" || true```
security-bot: Scan: searching S3 artifact buckets and recent build logs for pattern `AKIA` across all regions. ETA ~5m.
oncall-ryan: Containment done: rotated and disabled the two active access keys for ci-service-bot. Created new key for CI with least-privilege policy. Added temporary deny for older key ids.
Maria Gomez: good. please paste the key IDs and the IAM console link in #incidents once you can. Also create a KMS key rotation task if that key was used to encrypt secrets.
Ben Carter: results: artifact search found same key in 3 other archived logs (older nightly runs). We ran `aws s3api list-objects --bucket redwood-ci-artifacts --prefix nightly/ --query 'Contents[].Key'` then `s3 cp` and grep. No signs of exfil or external copy indicators in S3 access logs so far.
Jess Liu: I'll scrub the artifacts: mark the objects as deleted, replace with redacted copies, and update lifecycle to immediately expire these prefixes for 30 days.
Samir Patel: dev change: the packer recipes were running `aws configure --profile build` from a helper script that had debugging output on failure. fix is to stop printing creds, and add `set -o errexit` and `trap` to hide envs. Opening PR now.
Maria Gomez: post-incident items: add a CI-level secret masking rule, add a pre-commit hook to block `AKIA` patterns, add a step in builds to fail on uncovered secrets (exit code 2), and enable cloudtrail insights for S3 read anomalies.
oncall-ryan: I'll file the incident ticket and mark severity: high (potential secret leak). tagging legal/privacy for review.
Ben Carter: audit: running `git log --all --pretty=format:%H` + `git grep` scan across mirrors. I also ran the internal credential inventory to ensure key had no wide permissions. Policy check: key only allowed S3:GetObject and KMS:Decrypt for specific resources.
Jess Liu: can we add KMS envelope rotation? if the key decrypted anything with customer data we need to escalate. Maria, do you want to own KMS audit?
Maria Gomez: I'll take KMS audit. if we find ciphertext that used that principal, we'll rotate the KMS CMK and re-encrypt. putting that in the incident ticket.
Samir Patel: notify platform users? the service account is used by infra pipelines only; no customer-facing tokens. recommend limited external comms: internal incident note + brief to infra owners.
security-bot: Actions recorded: key revoked, artifacts redacted, PR opened to remove debug prints, CI secret-scan rule added (temporary), incident ticket created: INC-2026-042.
Jess Liu: follow-ups: 1) full retro with timeline, 2) expand CI masking to artifact storage pre-upload, 3) add audit log alert for future `aws configure` prints in logs, 4) training for infra devs. assigning owners in ticket.
Samir Patel: thanks everyone. will post the PR link and the raw log snippet (redacted) to the ticket. planning a 30-min postmortem tomorrow 15:00 UTC.
oncall-ryan: added to calendar. closing slack thread for now, update in ticket when KMS audit completes. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Monica Patel: looping in #eng-security for review on SDK parity work: auth env var naming + precedence + secret handling/redaction. We want one standard across Python/TS/Go.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed env vars (new + canonical):
- `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
- `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (for service-to-service tokens)
Legacy vars to deprecate but still read for a bit: `RW_API_KEY`, `REDWOOD_TOKEN`, `REDWOOD_KEY` (varies by SDK today)
Precedence proposal: explicit client param > env vars (canonical first, then legacy) > error.
Also: ensure we never log secrets, even in debug.

Marcus Lin: thx. couple initial reactions:
1) naming: +1 on `REDWOOD_API_KEY`. For token, prefer `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` over `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (people copy/paste “Bearer …” into env vars and we end up double-prefixing).
2) precedence: agree explicit > env. If multiple env vars are set, we need deterministic selection AND a warning (but warning must not include values).
3) redaction: hard requirement: do not print auth header, do not print raw env var contents, do not include secret fragments in exceptions by default.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 on “don’t bake Bearer into the env var name”. On the wire we still send `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.
Also we’ve seen customers accidentally set `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN="Bearer abc..."`.

Dana Lo: TS SDK today accepts `REDWOOD_API_KEY` + `REDWOOD_TOKEN` and logs the resolved headers in `debug=true` mode (oops). We’re removing header logging anyway, but want to confirm what’s allowed.

Marcus Lin: logging guidance:
- ok: log *presence* and source, e.g. `auth_source=env:REDWOOD_API_KEY` or `auth_source=explicit`
- ok: log whether auth header was set (boolean)
- ok: log request_id, status, error_code
- NOT ok: log header values (`Authorization`, `X-Api-Key`, etc), env var values, stack traces that include request config dumps.
If you have a “verbose http” option, it must either redact or be opt-in with a scary warning and still redact auth.

Sabrina James: Python SDK has a `debug` option that currently prints the full request headers for 4xx/5xx. We can strip `Authorization` and any `*key*`/`*token*` header names.
Question: is last-4 visible acceptable? e.g. `rwk_****abcd`?

Marcus Lin: prefer: no partials in default logs/exceptions. People forward logs to vendors/tickets.
If we *need* partial for support, make it an explicit callback/hook so customer chooses.
Compromise: maybe in *local* debug only, but I’d rather we avoid specifying “last 4” as a standard.

Allison Grant: from SRE side we mainly need:
- request_id
- retry_count
- timeout_ms
- endpoint/method
No need for key fragments.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SDK: we don’t log headers by default, but we have an `HTTPClient` hook some folks use that may log everything. We can add a `redwood.RedactingRoundTripper` wrapper and make it default in `Debug` mode.

Logan Wright: telemetry/logging schema we’re drafting includes `auth_source` (enum), `auth_type` (api_key|access_token), but never the secret itself.

Ibrahim Khan: ok, so on env var names: are we aligned on `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN`?

Marcus Lin: yes, that’s much better.
Also please document that the value should be the raw token string (no “Bearer ” prefix). SDK should `.trim()` and if it detects it starts with `Bearer `, treat as user error and emit safe message.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. We can do:
- if env value matches `^Bearer\s+` => strip? or error?
I’d prefer error with guidance to remove prefix.

Marcus Lin: error (safe): “Token should not include 'Bearer ' prefix. Provide the raw token value.”
Do not echo the provided value.

Dana Lo: precedence when both are set: if `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` are both present, which wins?

Monica Patel: my initial thought was API key wins (more common), but we can pick token wins if it’s “more privileged”? unsure.

Marcus Lin: security POV: deterministic + explicitness. I’d recommend:
- if explicit parameter provided, use it (and do not fall back)
- else if exactly one of the canonical env vars is set, use it
- else if both canonical are set, FAIL with a clear error asking customer to set only one (or pass explicitly)
Reason: silent selection leads to “why am I authenticating as the wrong principal” incidents.

Ibrahim Khan: that’s stricter than what we originally planned but makes sense.
Does that apply to legacy vars too?

Marcus Lin: yes. If multiple possible sources are set, fail fast. Exception: if you have a single canonical + a legacy alias that is literally the same concept, you can choose canonical and warn (again, no values). But avoid ambiguous states.

Sabrina James: for migration: Python users sometimes have `REDWOOD_TOKEN` set globally in CI and then pass api_key explicitly in code. Under this rule, explicit param wins, so ok.
But if they have both env vars set and *no* explicit param, they get an error. That’s acceptable?

Monica Patel: we can message in migration notes: “If you have both set, unset one.”

Allison Grant: +1 to failing fast. Better than accidentally using the wrong auth + getting 401s.

Mateusz Zielinski: implementation detail: Go currently accepts `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and sends `Authorization: Bearer <key>` (same header for both). We don’t have a separate header.
Is it acceptable that both api keys and access tokens map to `Authorization: Bearer`?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, backend treats both as bearer credentials; type is inferred server-side.
SDK can label `auth_type` based on which env var was used, but on-wire header is same.

Marcus Lin: ok, but don’t attempt to “validate” format in SDK beyond basic checks (non-empty, no whitespace-only, no Bearer prefix). Don’t regex-match prefixes like `rwk_` and then log it.

Dana Lo: redaction requirements for errors: TS currently throws `AuthenticationError: Missing API key, got: ${process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY}` (!!!). We’ll fix.
Should we also ensure HTTP libraries don’t include headers in thrown errors?

Marcus Lin: yes. Specifically:
- do not include request config objects in error messages
- if you wrap errors, ensure you’re not serializing the request
Add tests that simulate an error and assert secret is not present anywhere in `.message` or `.toString()`.

Ibrahim Khan: action items I’m taking from this thread:
- Rename token env var to `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` (and keep legacy `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN`/`REDWOOD_TOKEN` temporarily w/ deprecation warning)
- If both api_key + access_token env vars set and no explicit param => throw “ambiguous auth configuration” error
- Detect “Bearer ” prefix in env var values => throw safe guidance error
- Ensure debug logging never prints auth headers or env var values; add redaction wrappers where needed
- Add unit + conformance tests for redaction (scan exception/log output)

Marcus Lin: +1. Please also add doc text: “Never commit keys; use env vars / secret manager; SDK logs redact secrets by default.”
Tag me on the ADR/Confluence page when drafted, I’ll do final signoff.

Monica Patel: will do. thanks all.

Logan Wright: once names are final, I’ll update telemetry schema enum values (`env:redwood_api_key`, `env:redwood_access_token`, `explicit`).

Marcus Lin: cool. closing note: if you add any “diagnostic dump” feature, it must have a redaction allowlist (not a denylist) so we don’t miss new headers later.

Ibrahim Khan: noted — we’ll do allowlist (method, url, status, request_id) and never include headers by default.

title: #eng-security: 1767873008
preview:
Monica Patel: looping in #eng-security for review on SDK parity work: auth env var naming + precedence + secret handling/redaction. We want one standard across Python/TS/Go.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed env vars (new + canonical):
- `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
- `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (for service-to-service tokens)
Legacy vars to deprecate but still read for a bit: `RW_API_KEY`, `REDWOOD_TOKEN`, `REDWOOD_KEY` (varies by SDK today)
Precedence proposal: explicit client param > env vars (canonical first, then legacy) > error.
Also: ensure we never log secrets, even in debug.

Marcus Lin: thx. couple initial reactions:
1) naming: +1 on `REDWOOD_API_KEY`. For token, prefer `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` over `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (people copy/paste “Bearer …” into env vars and we end up double-prefixing).
2) precedence: agree explicit > env. If multiple env vars are set, we need deterministic selection AND a warning (but warning must not include values).
3) redaction: hard requirement: do not print auth header, do not print raw env var contents, do not include secret fragments in exceptions by default.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 on “don’t bake Bearer into the env var name”. On the wire we still send `Authorization: Bearer <token>`.
Also we’ve seen customers accidentally set `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN="Bearer abc..."`.

Dana Lo: TS SDK today accepts `REDWOOD_API_KEY` + `REDWOOD_TOKEN` and logs the resolved headers in `debug=true` mode (oops). We’re removing header logging anyway, but want to confirm what’s allowed.

Marcus Lin: logging guidance:
- ok: log *presence* and source, e.g. `auth_source=env:REDWOOD_API_KEY` or `auth_source=explicit`
- ok: log whether auth header was set (boolean)
- ok: log request_id, status, error_code
- NOT ok: log header values (`Authorization`, `X-Api-Key`, etc), env var values, stack traces that include request config dumps.
If you have a “verbose http” option, it must either redact or be opt-in with a scary warning and still redact auth.

Sabrina James: Python SDK has a `debug` option that currently prints the full request headers for 4xx/5xx. We can strip `Authorization` and any `*key*`/`*token*` header names.
Question: is last-4 visible acceptable? e.g. `rwk_****abcd`?

Marcus Lin: prefer: no partials in default logs/exceptions. People forward logs to vendors/tickets.
If we *need* partial for support, make it an explicit callback/hook so customer chooses.
Compromise: maybe in *local* debug only, but I’d rather we avoid specifying “last 4” as a standard.

Allison Grant: from SRE side we mainly need:
- request_id
- retry_count
- timeout_ms
- endpoint/method
No need for key fragments.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SDK: we don’t log headers by default, but we have an `HTTPClient` hook some folks use that may log everything. We can add a `redwood.RedactingRoundTripper` wrapper and make it default in `Debug` mode.

Logan Wright: telemetry/logging schema we’re drafting includes `auth_source` (enum), `auth_type` (api_key|access_token), but never the secret itself.

Ibrahim Khan: ok, so on env var names: are we aligned on `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN`?

Marcus Lin: yes, that’s much better.
Also please document that the value should be the raw token string (no “Bearer ” prefix). SDK should `.trim()` and if it detects it starts with `Bearer `, treat as user error and emit safe message.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. We can do:
- if env value matches `^Bearer\s+` => strip? or error?
I’d prefer error with guidance to remove prefix.

Marcus Lin: error (safe): “Token should not include 'Bearer ' prefix. Provide the raw token value.”
Do not echo the provided value.

Dana Lo: precedence when both are set: if `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` are both present, which wins?

Monica Patel: my initial thought was API key wins (more common), but we can pick token wins if it’s “more privileged”? unsure.

Marcus Lin: security POV: deterministic + explicitness. I’d recommend:
- if explicit parameter provided, use it (and do not fall back)
- else if exactly one of the canonical env vars is set, use it
- else if both canonical are set, FAIL with a clear error asking customer to set only one (or pass explicitly)
Reason: silent selection leads to “why am I authenticating as the wrong principal” incidents.

Ibrahim Khan: that’s stricter than what we originally planned but makes sense.
Does that apply to legacy vars too?

Marcus Lin: yes. If multiple possible sources are set, fail fast. Exception: if you have a single canonical + a legacy alias that is literally the same concept, you can choose canonical and warn (again, no values). But avoid ambiguous states.

Sabrina James: for migration: Python users sometimes have `REDWOOD_TOKEN` set globally in CI and then pass api_key explicitly in code. Under this rule, explicit param wins, so ok.
But if they have both env vars set and *no* explicit param, they get an error. That’s acceptable?

Monica Patel: we can message in migration notes: “If you have both set, unset one.”

Allison Grant: +1 to failing fast. Better than accidentally using the wrong auth + getting 401s.

Mateusz Zielinski: implementation detail: Go currently accepts `REDWOOD_API_KEY` and sends `Authorization: Bearer <key>` (same header for both). We don’t have a separate header.
Is it acceptable that both api keys and access tokens map to `Authorization: Bearer`?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, backend treats both as bearer credentials; type is inferred server-side.
SDK can label `auth_type` based on which env var was used, but on-wire header is same.

Marcus Lin: ok, but don’t attempt to “validate” format in SDK beyond basic checks (non-empty, no whitespace-only, no Bearer prefix). Don’t regex-match prefixes like `rwk_` and then log it.

Dana Lo: redaction requirements for errors: TS currently throws `AuthenticationError: Missing API key, got: ${process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY}` (!!!). We’ll fix.
Should we also ensure HTTP libraries don’t include headers in thrown errors?

Marcus Lin: yes. Specifically:
- do not include request config objects in error messages
- if you wrap errors, ensure you’re not serializing the request
Add tests that simulate an error and assert secret is not present anywhere in `.message` or `.toString()`.

Ibrahim Khan: action items I’m taking from this thread:
- Rename token env var to `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` (and keep legacy `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN`/`REDWOOD_TOKEN` temporarily w/ deprecation warning)
- If both api_key + access_token env vars set and no explicit param => throw “ambiguous auth configuration” error
- Detect “Bearer ” prefix in env var values => throw safe guidance error
- Ensure debug logging never prints auth headers or env var values; add redaction wrappers where needed
- Add unit + conformance tests for redaction (scan exception/log output)

Marcus Lin: +1. Please also add doc text: “Never commit keys; use env vars / secret manager; SDK logs redact secrets by default.”
Tag me on the ADR/Confluence page when drafted, I’ll do final signoff.

Monica Patel: will do. thanks all.

Logan Wright: once names are final, I’ll update telemetry schema enum values (`env:redwood_api_key`, `env:redwood_access_token`, `explicit`).

Marcus Lin: cool. closing note: if you add any “diagnostic dump” feature, it must have a redaction allowlist (not a denylist) so we don’t miss new headers later.

Ibrahim Khan: noted — we’ll do allowlist (method, url, status, request_id) and never include headers by default.

source.thread:
Nina Gupta: quick sync idea: introduce a lightweight token-broker service that exchanges legacy API keys for ephemeral runtime tokens and also emits a numeric registry id for gateways. goal: no per-request RBAC remote eval.
Ethan Park: sounds good. token-broker sits in authn plane, signs minimal JWTs for gateways. cache-friendly claims only (sub, rid, bv, exp).
Rhea Singh: +1. need to define rid (registry id) semantics. should be stable for short windows and rotate on membership changes.
Matt Berger: for Dedicated/Private installs, brokers can run locally as an agent too. agent issues tokens from local registry and serves delta feed.
Nina Gupta: migration: when presented with legacy key, broker validates and returns ephemeral token w/ rid mapping. also emit audit event with mapping. we can TTL-map old keys gradually.
Ethan Park: gateway flow: verify signature or session cache, lookup rid in local cache -> apply precomputed ACL. on miss, fetch bundle by bv or request delta.
Rhea Singh: bundle scheme: compressed protobuf with role defs, route table, and param schemas (for dataset-scoped perms). signed by control plane. gateways validate signature + bundle_version match in token.
Matt Berger: perf ops: avoid per-token signature verify where possible. maintain LRU of verified token ids for a short window; still verify signature on new tokens.
policy-bot: added runbook task "broker smoke + agent local-run tests" https://redwood.internal/runbooks/token-broker
Nina Gupta: action items: Rhea draft bundle proto + signing header, Ethan bench gateway session cache, Matt sketch agent mode, I'll draft adapter sequence diagram. RFC follow-up Friday 9am.
Ethan Park: fine by me. I'll aim for numbers showing <10ms extra on hot path.
Rhea Singh: will share proto by EOD Thurs.
Matt Berger: will validate agent startup fingerprint for bundle integrity.
Nina Gupta: thanks team. keeping runtime tokens minimal + bundle/delta feed for offline enforcement should cover Hosted and Private product needs.

Kevin Osei: Picking up the audit event schema review for offline bundle verification outcomes. Goal: nail the fields + redaction rules before we lock `audit-log-exporter/pr-3112`. Can folks sanity check the proposed schema + what we should NOT log?

Kevin Osei: Current draft (names wip):
```
event_name: "private.bundle_verification"
event_version: "1"
timestamp: <RFC3339>
severity: INFO|WARN|ERROR
outcome: SUCCESS|FAILURE
failure_reason: <enum>
verification_stage: preflight|install|upgrade
bundle_build_id: <string>
bundle_version: <string>
manifest_digest: "sha256:<hex>"
signature_digest: "sha256:<hex>"  # detached sig file digest
signing_key_id: <string>           # short key identifier
trust_store_version: <string>
installer_version: <string>
downgrade_prevented: true|false
break_glass_used: true|false
break_glass_ticket: <string|null>
time_source: system|override
host_id: <string>                  # local-only? maybe hashed
node_role: installer|controller
redaction_applied: true|false
```
Main question: do we include bundle path / file names? And should `host_id` ever leave the cluster?

Logan Wright: +1 on keeping it tight. From installer side we can populate most of that. Bundle path is tricky; support *asks* for it but it’s basically customer filesystem layout.

Allison Grant: Re: path — our logging standard treats full paths as potentially sensitive (customer usernames, mount points, project names). If we need it, we should either (a) omit, (b) log only basename, or (c) hash + label as such.

Ethan Park: Installer currently knows:
- bundle root path
- manifest filename
- signature filename
- list of artifacts in manifest
We definitely should NOT log the full artifact list, it can include image names, chart names, etc.

Marcus Lin: Agree on no artifact list. Threat model item TM-7 was “avoid leaking internal topology or customer-specific naming via logs”. Paths and artifact refs are both under that.

Caleb Johnson: On exporter side, we can mark certain fields as `local_only` and not forward via SIEM exporters. But we need to be explicit in schema docs, otherwise customers will assume everything is exported.

Kevin Osei: Good call. Maybe we split into “audit event” vs “diagnostic blob”. Audit event should be minimal + exportable; diagnostics go to support bundle only.

Dev Patel: From Support POV, path is only useful for “you pointed installer at the wrong directory” type issues. But we can get that from support bundle instead of audit events.

Logan Wright: We already include `bundle_root` in support bundle metadata (PR-25011 wiring). We can keep audit clean.

Hanae Suzuki: For `signing_key_id`: please do not log full KMS key ARN or cert subject. Use a Redwood key alias like `rwi-private-offline-2026q1` or a short fingerprint.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: if we include a fingerprint, be consistent: `ed25519:<hex8>` or `spki_sha256:<b64>`.

Allison Grant: Another thing: `host_id` is almost always considered customer environment identifier. If we include it, it should be salted hash generated locally, and default should be “not exported”.

Caleb Johnson: We can set mapping defaults:
- `host_id_hash` stays local
- exporter drops it unless customer explicitly configures allowlist
But we need a stable value for correlation across events on a single node.

Ethan Park: Works. Installer can compute `host_id_hash = sha256(machine-id + local_salt)`.

Kevin Osei: Concern: machine-id might be missing / unreliable in some hardened builds.

Ethan Park: True. We can fall back to generated UUID stored in installer state directory. We already have a local install state file for downgrade prevention.

Marcus Lin: That’s preferable anyway. Avoid collecting hardware identifiers.

Kevin Osei: Ok so `host_id_hash` derived from installer state UUID.

AuditLogBot: Reminder: schema change PR open: https://github.com/redwood/audit-log-exporter/pull/3112 (review requested)

Caleb Johnson: On `failure_reason` enum, can we align with installer error taxonomy thread? We should not have freeform strings.

Logan Wright: Installer error codes are like:
- `SIG_INVALID`
- `SIG_MISSING`
- `MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH`
- `ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH`
- `UNKNOWN_KEY`
- `KEY_REVOKED`
- `CERT_NOT_YET_VALID`
- `CERT_EXPIRED`
- `DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED`
- `BREAK_GLASS_REQUIRED`
- `IO_READ_ERROR`
We can map to that.

Kevin Osei: Great. Also want `failure_detail`? (short string) or keep it out.

Allison Grant: Please avoid `failure_detail` if it can accidentally include paths, filenames, or raw error messages. If we need more context, include a constrained `remediation_hint_code`.

Dev Patel: +1. Support gets burned when customers copy/paste raw error with sensitive pathing into tickets anyway, but at least we shouldn’t broadcast it in audit logs.

Marcus Lin: Another redaction topic: `break_glass_ticket` might include internal Jira IDs (SUP-/INT-). That’s not customer safe if exported.

Kevin Osei: Good catch. We can make `break_glass_ticket` optional + local-only. For exported audit event maybe just `break_glass_used=true` and `break_glass_approval_recorded=true/false`.

Hanae Suzuki: For break-glass, we also wanted to record *who* invoked it. Are we doing user identity here?

Allison Grant: Identity is sensitive and depends on auth mode. If available, log a pseudonymous `actor_id` (like installer local user) only to local audit, not export. Exported should not include user unless customer explicitly enables it.

Ethan Park: Installer can record `actor` from:
- CLI user (if present)
- or “unknown”
But in air-gapped, often run as root via scripts.

Marcus Lin: I’d rather not include actor in v1. Break-glass is already high-signal. If we need actor, make it opt-in and document it.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Exporter will be much simpler.

Kevin Osei: Ok. Proposed changes:
- Remove bundle path + filenames from audit event schema (keep in support bundle)
- `host_id_hash` local-only, derived from installer state UUID
- `signing_key_id` is Redwood alias/fingerprint, not KMS ARN
- `failure_reason` constrained enum aligned to installer error codes
- No `failure_detail` freeform; maybe `remediation_hint_code`
- Break-glass: exported includes only booleans; internal ticket/actor local-only

Logan Wright: Works for me. I’ll update installer emission accordingly.

Allison Grant: Please add `redaction_applied=true` only when we actually drop/transform fields. Otherwise it becomes meaningless.

Kevin Osei: Makes sense. Maybe we rename to `data_minimized=true` and keep it always true for exported schema.

Marcus Lin: Also ensure we don’t log manifest digest + signature digest if we consider them “sensitive”. I think they’re ok (integrity-only), but confirm.

Hanae Suzuki: Hashes are fine. They can’t be reversed and are useful for “you have bundle X” vs “bundle Y”.

Caleb Johnson: Hashes also help correlate with the signed manifest in our release system.

Kevin Osei: Cool. I’ll comment on PR-3112 with these decisions + ask for schema update. Thanks all.

Dev Patel: When you update schema, can we add a short note in the docs: “for troubleshooting, use `installer bundle inspect` and attach support bundle; audit logs are intentionally minimal”. That reduces customer confusion.

Ethan Park: +1. We’ve had folks treat audit logs as debug logs.

Kevin Osei: Will do. Dropping summary in thread + pinging Docs folks separately.

source.body:
Fatima Noor: Got a Q from Helios Defense on today’s call: can they use *their* PKI root / customer-signed bundles instead of trusting a Redwood signing cert? They’re saying policy prohibits installing anything unless it chains to their internal root.

Marcus Lin: Ugh, yeah, we’ve heard variants of this. They’re basically asking for customer-controlled trust root or delegation. Do they mean “we verify Redwood signature AND also we sign internally after receipt” or literally “Redwood ships unsigned and customer signs”? Big difference.

Fatima Noor: They want the installer to accept bundles that are signed by them (their offline release team) because the artifact has to go through their internal approval/signing gateway before it can cross into the air-gap.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: if we allow arbitrary customer signatures, we’re effectively letting them bypass Redwood provenance unless we do a dual-sign / co-sign flow. Also creates support nightmares (“why does verify fail” / different algorithms).

Hanae Suzuki: Technically: we can support adding extra trust roots (trust store file) but that still doesn’t solve “customer signs instead of Redwood”. To do “customer signs”, installer would need to treat customer root as equivalent to Redwood root, which is a big policy shift.

Kevin Osei: Also audit angle: if customer signature is the only trust anchor, we can’t attest integrity/provenance of what they installed vs what we released. Our audit events would show “verified” but it’s not a Redwood signature.

Marcus Lin: Exactly. Threat model wise, the whole point is: signed by Redwood keys, verified offline, fail closed. Customer-sign-only opens supply-chain gaps (they could accidentally sign a tampered bundle, we’d never know). Even dual-sign changes the story and we need crisp semantics in logs.

Fatima Noor: Customer says they’re okay with dual-sign: Redwood signs, then they re-sign “for internal compliance”. They want installer to accept their signature because their policy tooling might strip/rehydrate packages.

Ethan Park: From installer perspective we can support:

title: #eng-security: 1760784422
preview:
Fatima Noor: Got a Q from Helios Defense on today’s call: can they use *their* PKI root / customer-signed bundles instead of trusting a Redwood signing cert? They’re saying policy prohibits installing anything unless it chains to their internal root.

Marcus Lin: Ugh, yeah, we’ve heard variants of this. They’re basically asking for customer-controlled trust root or delegation. Do they mean “we verify Redwood signature AND also we sign internally after receipt” or literally “Redwood ships unsigned and customer signs”? Big difference.

Fatima Noor: They want the installer to accept bundles that are signed by them (their offline release team) because the artifact has to go through their internal approval/signing gateway before it can cross into the air-gap.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: if we allow arbitrary customer signatures, we’re effectively letting them bypass Redwood provenance unless we do a dual-sign / co-sign flow. Also creates support nightmares (“why does verify fail” / different algorithms).

Hanae Suzuki: Technically: we can support adding extra trust roots (trust store file) but that still doesn’t solve “customer signs instead of Redwood”. To do “customer signs”, installer would need to treat customer root as equivalent to Redwood root, which is a big policy shift.

Kevin Osei: Also audit angle: if customer signature is the only trust anchor, we can’t attest integrity/provenance of what they installed vs what we released. Our audit events would show “verified” but it’s not a Redwood signature.

Marcus Lin: Exactly. Threat model wise, the whole point is: signed by Redwood keys, verified offline, fail closed. Customer-sign-only opens supply-chain gaps (they could accidentally sign a tampered bundle, we’d never know). Even dual-sign changes the story and we need crisp semantics in logs.

Fatima Noor: Customer says they’re okay with dual-sign: Redwood signs, then they re-sign “for internal compliance”. They want installer to accept their signature because their policy tooling might strip/rehydrate packages.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: We should be careful not to over-promise. Security position: Redwood-signed bundles are mandatory. Future: we can explore customer *co-signing* as an additional validation step, but not as a replacement. Please document as a “future consideration” with constraints.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll add a note to the PRD/FAQ: “Customer-managed signing roots / customer-signed bundles: not supported in v1; roadmap item = co-signing or configurable secondary trust store.”

Ethan Park: If we write it down, please be explicit that trust store config is only for Redwood key rotation / multiple Redwood public keys. Not a general “bring your own root” right now.

Olga Petrov: +1. Also add to customer security questionnaire response template: mandatory Redwood signature verification; additional customer controls allowed but don’t replace.

Kevin Osei: Should we open a Linear ticket so it doesn’t get lost? Something like “Customer co-sign / secondary signature verification policy”.

Marcus Lin: Yes. I’ll file it under Security/Private follow-ups. Not committing timeline.

Fatima Noor: Great, thanks. I’ll relay to Helios: they can wrap-sign the *bundle tarball* for internal governance, but must preserve our included manifest + signature and verify with our installer.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please also tell them: if their process requires content mutation (repackaging), they’ll need an exception on their side or we’ll need a design engagement later. We are intentionally fail-closed on any modifications.

Connor O'Brien: (side note) This comes up with AsterBank too. Might be worth adding a short section in docs: “How to move bundles through internal gateways without breaking verification.”

Hanae Suzuki: I can draft a snippet: recommended commands for checksums before/after transfer + warning about re-zipping.

Marcus Lin: Cool. Action items:
- Marcus: create follow-up ticket + add blurb to threat model as “deferred feature / risk”.
- Connor: PRD/FAQ note.
- Fatima: customer response.

Kevin Osei: And I’ll make sure audit event schema doesn’t paint us into a corner if we add “secondary signature verified” later.

Ethan Park: +1. If/when we do it, it should be additive fields in audit log, not a new event type.

Fatima Noor: Thanks all.
