- `ri_plg_signal_window` (string: “7d”, “30d”)
- `ri_plg_suppressed_until` (timestamp)
Timeline note: “PLG signal: Reliability risk (429s sustained). Recommend Dedicated eval. Evidence: 429s> X/day for 5 of last 7 days.”
No raw counts unless we agree.

Olga Petrov: For SOC2 + least privilege, we should ensure properties are not editable by everyone (esp. any free-form text fields). Also ensure audit logs exist for who/what wrote the properties.

David Kim: HubSpot supports property-level permissions and can restrict editing/visibility by teams, but it’s not perfect for “security-only.” Usually we can:
- lock properties to Admin write (integration user)
- visibility to Sales/CS only (exclude broader Marketing if needed)
- keep the timeline note short + templated
Also we can keep sensitive details out of notes (notes get copied around).

Tyler Benson: Marketing ask: please don’t drop anything that could be interpreted as “tracking users.” If we do docs intent, keep it category-level and aggregated, not page-level paths, and don’t include query params.

Elliot Price: Docs instrumentation currently captures page category + workspace (if logged in) OR anonymous session if not. For signals, we can require “known workspace” only (i.e., must be authenticated and mapped to an org). And we can bucket pages into coarse categories: Security FAQ, Dedicated docs, Private/VPC docs, Pricing.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Requirement from privacy perspective:
1) Intent signals must be category-level, aggregated, and require a known workspace/org.
2) Do not send the specific doc URL, nor the visitor identity.
3) Minimum threshold before routing (avoid “vanity signal” + reduces creepiness).

Nikhil Sharma: On thresholds: MVP intent signal triggers only if (Security FAQ OR Private deployment docs) viewed ≥3 times over 14d AND org has >N tokens in last 30d OR has added >2 workspaces. Trying to avoid random browsing.

Marcus Lin: Can we include “evidence fields” like “429_count_7d=350” in HubSpot? It helps AEs not ask dumb questions.

Michael Grant: Be careful. Counts themselves aren’t personal, but they are potentially sensitive business data. If included, keep them coarse (ranges) and avoid per-day granularity. Better: “high/medium/low” severity and a short explanation.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Agree with Legal. I’d prefer:
- severity banding (e.g., spend_velocity: high)
- “why” text that is templated and doesn’t include exact numbers
If we must include a number, keep it capped/rounded and ensure it’s only at the company level.

Logan Wright: Easy to do. We can map raw metrics → bands:
- spend_velocity_band: low/med/high
- rate_limit_pressure_band: low/med/high (derived from 429s per request)
- growth_band: steady / accelerating
Then HubSpot note uses those.

Caleb Johnson: Also: make sure we’re not forwarding request IDs, IPs, or error payloads. Some 429 logs include trace IDs that can be correlated. I’ll add a lint/check in the router to block those fields.

SecurityBot: Reminder: Data classification policy applies to CRM exports. Avoid storing customer secrets, tokens, or end-user identifiers in HubSpot. If unsure, default to not sending.

Olga Petrov: Retention: what are we planning for (a) raw events, (b) aggregated dataset, (c) HubSpot properties/notes?

Mei Lin: Current default in analytics:
- raw telemetry events: 30 days
- aggregated daily tables: 13 months (for seasonality)
We can adjust for PLG signals specifically.

Marcus Lin: For HubSpot, I don’t think we can enforce TTL on timeline notes automatically. Properties we can overwrite; notes persist.

David Kim: Correct. Notes are effectively permanent unless manually deleted or via API cleanup job. We can implement a cleanup job keyed on `hubspot_company_id` + a “delete requested” flag, but not true automatic TTL unless we build it.

Michael Grant: Then we should avoid putting anything in notes that we wouldn’t want retained indefinitely. Put details in our internal analytics; in HubSpot keep to a minimal summary + link to internal dashboard (access-controlled).

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s the right pattern:
- HubSpot: minimal, non-sensitive summary + routing metadata
- Internal: full signal breakdown behind RBAC
Also ensure we have a documented deletion path for customer requests (DSR).

Jordan Blake: From CRO side: as long as reps can understand “why now” and what to do next, we can live without raw numbers. Don’t slow rollout, but do it clean.

Marcus Lin: Access controls proposal:
- HubSpot properties created under “Redwood PLG Signals” group
- write access: integration user only
- read access: Sales + CS + Sales Ops (exclude broad Marketing by default)
- timeline note template: fixed phrases only, no free-form evidence
Everyone ok?

Tyler Benson: +1 excluding Marketing by default. If we need it later, we can add a sanitized “lifecycle segment” property for nurture.

Olga Petrov: Add to SOC2 evidence: list of fields in/out, retention, and access control configuration. Also document the approval checkpoints.

Marcus Lin: Action items for Confluence data handling page (owner tbd):
1) Enumerate fields sent to HubSpot (ALLOWLIST) + explicit denylist (no PII, no doc URLs, no IPs, no prompts).
2) Retention: raw 30d, aggregates 13mo, HubSpot notes = “no TTL” → keep minimal; properties overwritten; cleanup process for DSR.
3) Access: property permissions (read/write), who can view signal history, audit logs.
4) Deletion request runbook: how to locate company + delete notes/properties + remove from analytics tables.
5) Suppression rules to prevent repeated outreach + opt-out handling.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Assign owners: @Marcus Lin please draft section (1) + denylist. @Mei Lin add retention + dataset storage details. @David Kim add HubSpot permissions + integration user setup. I’ll review and sign off.

Mei Lin: Sounds good. I’ll include the table names + retention configs, plus a note that we only store org/workspace IDs and aggregate windows.

David Kim: I’ll document the HubSpot property group, permission settings, and which teams can read it. Also will add integration audit trail notes.

Michael Grant: Please include a sentence that intent signals are derived from first-party product/docs usage and aggregated at account level; no cross-site tracking; and we provide opt-out if requested.

Nikhil Sharma: I’ll add the “minimum thresholds” and “no vanity signals” rationale to the PRD + link it from the data handling doc.

Marcus Lin: Great. I’ll post draft in Confluence by EOD tomorrow and tag for review. Also I’ll add a unit test in router to enforce the allowlist and fail closed if unknown fields appear.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Final note: for EU accounts, confirm we’re not moving any personal data cross-region. Since this is company-level metadata, should be fine, but document that signals do not include end-user data and are stored per our standard CRM processing terms.

Marcus Lin: Got it. Closing loop once the Confluence page is up + reviewed.

source.messages:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off review for Private min-pack log policy + redaction. Can someone paste the current structured fields we’re planning to ship (control plane + data plane)? Main question from Sec: are we logging any request payload/prompt/completion at all?

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime (data plane) we’re aligning to the structured fields from pr-18521. Current plan is (high level): `timestamp, level, service, component, request_id, trace_id, span_id, route, model, status_code, error_type, latency_ms, input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, batch_id (optional), kv_cache_hit (bool), gpu_id (optional), pod, node`. Plus `tenant_id_hash` (not raw tenant) and `client_app` if provided.

Naomi Feldman: Thanks. Need to be super explicit: do we log *any* prompt text, completion text, tool/function args, or request bodies? Even “debug logs” sometimes sneak this in.

Omar El-Sayed: Default is NO prompt/completion/body logging. We currently have a couple of code paths that log “request payload” on 5xx in dev builds; those are being removed / guarded behind a compile-time flag. In prod/private builds we’ll only log sizes + hashes.

Logan Wright: +1. Telemetry side: we can add `prompt_sha256` (computed over normalized prompt) BUT I’d prefer not shipping that by default either; it’s still potentially correlatable if customers reuse prompts. Better: `payload_bytes` + token counts, and a `request_shape` enum (chat vs completion vs embeddings etc).

Kevin Osei: Agree. Hashes can become identifiers. Also: we should treat IP addresses and user identifiers as personal data in some jurisdictions. What’s the plan for `remote_addr` / `x-forwarded-for` / `user_id`?

Diego Santos: In Private installs the gateway can be in front of customer’s LB; we currently see `x-forwarded-for` at the gateway. Some customers want it for abuse debugging. We can:
1) drop it by default
2) optionally keep a truncated /24 (v4) or /64 (v6)
3) allow full IP only when customer explicitly turns it on.

Allison Grant: From standards angle (Private metrics + logs): keep cardinality bounded and avoid identity labels/fields. Logging full IPs can be high volume + privacy risk. I’d vote default “no IP”, but keep a `client_network` coarse bucket if needed.

Marcus Lin: Let’s write that into the policy. Another big one: headers. Are we logging request headers anywhere? (auth, cookies, org IDs, etc)

Nadia Rahman: Not in the normal request logs. But some libraries log headers at `DEBUG` when upstream calls fail (e.g., registry integration). We need a global redaction filter on the log forwarder side AND an app-side logger wrapper to enforce.

Colin O'Donnell: + customer angle: SIEM exports. Splunk folks frequently ask for headers to diagnose auth. We should give them a safe alternative: log `auth_scheme` and `auth_result` rather than `authorization` header. And a correlation id they can use.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed app-side redaction (before it hits stdout):
- Never emit: `authorization`, `proxy-authorization`, `cookie`, `set-cookie`, `x-api-key`, `x-auth-token`, `x-amz-security-token`
- Also redact any key matching `(?i)(token|secret|password|passwd|api[_-]?key|session)`
- For known IDs: `tenant_id` -> `tenant_id_hash`; `user_id` drop or hash (TBD)
- For URLs: strip query params (since those sometimes include tokens)

Naomi Feldman: Good. Two clarifications:
1) Hashing: what salt/key? If it’s stable across time, it’s still an identifier. If it’s per-install derived, ok, but we need to document.
2) Query params: stripping should be default; do not rely on teams “not putting secrets in query strings” :)

Logan Wright: For hashing: we can use HMAC-SHA256 with a per-install secret stored in Kubernetes Secret (installer generates). That gives consistent correlation within a cluster but not across customers. We can rotate if needed.

Kevin Osei: That works. Document that it’s “pseudonymous identifier” and may still be considered personal data. Also: do not log email addresses/usernames anywhere.

Marcus Lin: What about model/route fields? Those can leak sensitive info if customers encode tenant names into routes (seen it before). Do we need a warning / guidance?

Allison Grant: We can’t prevent customers from naming routes poorly, but we can:
- strongly recommend route names be non-identifying
- optionally support `route_id` (UUID) over freeform `route_name`
- cap route length and sanitize.

Omar El-Sayed: Today we log `route` as configured route name. We can add `route_id` and make `route` optional. For v1 minimum pack I can make default `route_id` only and allow `route` behind config.

Colin O'Donnell: Please do. This comes up in security reviews (“are you logging customer data?”). Route names *can* be customer data.

Naomi Feldman: +1. Same for `model` if they alias it with internal project names. Prefer `model_id` from registry.

Nadia Rahman: For runtime we have `model` as the serving “slug”. We can log `model_id` (registry UUID) and `model_family` (e.g., llama3) without customer alias. We should avoid printing full filesystem paths for on-prem models too.

Marcus Lin: Retention guidance: what are we recommending in the minimum pack? We need something that won’t violate customer policies but is “supportable.”

Diego Santos: Current installer defaults (proposal):
- operational logs: 7 days retention in Loki (if enabled), with size-based eviction
- Prometheus metrics: 14 days
- Grafana dashboards: n/a
But customers can point logs to Splunk/ELK and set their own retention.

Kevin Osei: 7 days default seems ok, but add language:
- customer is data controller in Private; they set retention
- Redwood recommends minimal retention required for troubleshooting
- avoid retaining raw request content (which we won’t log anyway)
Also align with audit logs doc: audit logs may require longer (90+ days) depending on compliance. Make sure we don’t conflate operational logs with audit logs.

Naomi Feldman: Yes, separate channels:
- Audit logs: security-relevant actions (auth events, config changes, user management, model publish/deploy actions)
- Operational logs: service health, errors, performance
Audit logs should never include request bodies either.

Omar El-Sayed: For request payload logging specifically:
- Default: DISABLED
- Not supported in “minimum pack” to turn on body logging globally
- If customer insists, we can provide an advanced debug mode that logs payloads only for a specific `request_id` allowlist and for a short TTL, but that’s probably premium / PS-only.

Marcus Lin: Put that in writing: “request/prompt/completion content is not logged by default and Redwood Support will not ask customers to enable it for troubleshooting unless approved by Security.”

Allison Grant: Also ensure alert annotations/runbooks don’t tell people to “grep logs for request body” etc.

Colin O'Donnell: Can we include an example of a compliant request log line in docs? Helps customers validate what’s being exported to SIEM.

Logan Wright: Example (sanitized) we can ship:
```json
{"ts":"2025-05-xxT12:34:56.789Z","level":"INFO","service":"runtime","component":"router","request_id":"req_01H...","trace_id":"4bf92f...","tenant_id_hash":"hmac256:9c1d...","route_id":"route_7b1...","model_id":"mdl_2f9...","status_code":200,"latency_ms":183,"input_tokens":512,"output_tokens":128,"total_tokens":640,"payload_bytes":8421,"request_shape":"chat.completions","kv_cache_hit":true}
```
No prompt/completion.

Naomi Feldman: Looks good. One more: error logs. Sometimes stack traces include env vars / config values. Are we filtering those?

Omar El-Sayed: Stack traces can contain URLs with credentials (rare but possible). We can add a regex scrubber to the collector (Vector/Fluent Bit) for common patterns like `user:pass@host` and `Bearer <token>`. But best is app-side: ensure config printers never include secret values.

Kevin Osei: Add explicit “do not log config dumps” guidance in runbooks. Also require redaction for:
- KMS key material identifiers? (those can be sensitive but usually ok)
- customer domain names? (borderline)
I think focus on secrets and PII.

Marcus Lin: Decision summary so far (please correct):
- No request payload/prompt/completion/tool args logged by default (and not part of supported min-pack troubleshooting)
- Headers never logged; explicit denylist + regex redact
- Strip query params from URLs
- `tenant_id` -> per-install HMAC hash; no raw user identifiers; no emails
- Default omit IP; optional truncated IP behind config
- Prefer `route_id` / `model_id` over names; names optional
- Retention defaults: 7 days operational logs (customer-controlled), audit logs separate with separate guidance

Naomi Feldman: Yup. Please add to doc: “If customer enables extended debug logging, they are responsible for validating content/retention. Redwood Support will advise reverting to defaults after incident.”

Diego Santos: Action items:
- Omar: update runtime + gateway logging defaults (route_id/model_id, drop route/model names)
- Logan: update collector config with redaction rules + URL query stripping
- Marcus/Naomi: I’ll draft the policy text for the Private min-pack docs and link it in the security review checklist

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: I’ll file a follow-up ticket for a quick validation test in the smoke harness: send a request with `Authorization: Bearer abc...` + query param `token=123` and assert it never shows up in logs.

Nadia Rahman: Great idea. Also test we don’t log any request body when a 500 is triggered.

Kevin Osei: Please loop me in on the retention wording. Some customers will ask “what does Redwood recommend” and we should be consistent.

Marcus Lin: Will do. Marking this thread resolved once the doc PR is up + redaction test is in CI.

2) Query params: stripping should be default; do not rely on teams “not putting secrets in query strings” :)

Logan Wright: For hashing: we can use HMAC-SHA256 with a per-install secret stored in Kubernetes Secret (installer generates). That gives consistent correlation within a cluster but not across customers. We can rotate if needed.

Kevin Osei: That works. Document that it’s “pseudonymous identifier” and may still be considered personal data. Also: do not log email addresses/usernames anywhere.

Marcus Lin: What about model/route fields? Those can leak sensitive info if customers encode tenant names into routes (seen it before). Do we need a warning / guidance?

Allison Grant: We can’t prevent customers from naming routes poorly, but we can:
- strongly recommend route names be non-identifying
- optionally support `route_id` (UUID) over freeform `route_name`
- cap route length and sanitize.

Omar El-Sayed: Today we log `route` as configured route name. We can add `route_id` and make `route` optional. For v1 minimum pack I can make default `route_id` only and allow `route` behind config.

Colin O'Donnell: Please do. This comes up in security reviews (“are you logging customer data?”). Route names *can* be customer data.

Naomi Feldman: +1. Same for `model` if they alias it with internal project names. Prefer `model_id` from registry.

Nadia Rahman: For runtime we have `model` as the serving “slug”. We can log `model_id` (registry UUID) and `model_family` (e.g., llama3) without customer alias. We should avoid printing full filesystem paths for on-prem models too.

Marcus Lin: Retention guidance: what are we recommending in the minimum pack? We need something that won’t violate customer policies but is “supportable.”

Diego Santos: Current installer defaults (proposal):
- operational logs: 7 days retention in Loki (if enabled), with size-based eviction
- Prometheus metrics: 14 days
- Grafana dashboards: n/a
But customers can point logs to Splunk/ELK and set their own retention.

Kevin Osei: 7 days default seems ok, but add language:
- customer is data controller in Private; they set retention
- Redwood recommends minimal retention required for troubleshooting
- avoid retaining raw request content (which we won’t log anyway)
Also align with audit logs doc: audit logs may require longer (90+ days) depending on compliance. Make sure we don’t conflate operational logs with audit logs.

Naomi Feldman: Yes, separate channels:
- Audit logs: security-relevant actions (auth events, config changes, user management, model publish/deploy actions)
- Operational logs: service health, errors, performance
Audit logs should never include request bodies either.

Omar El-Sayed: For request payload logging specifically:
- Default: DISABLED
- Not supported in “minimum pack” to turn on body logging globally
- If customer insists, we can provide an advanced debug mode that logs payloads only for a specific `request_id` allowlist and for a short TTL, but that’s probably premium / PS-only.

Marcus Lin: Put that in writing: “request/prompt/completion content is not logged by default and Redwood Support will not ask customers to enable it for troubleshooting unless approved by Security.”

Allison Grant: Also ensure alert annotations/runbooks don’t tell people to “grep logs for request body” etc.

Colin O'Donnell: Can we include an example of a compliant request log line in docs? Helps customers validate what’s being exported to SIEM.

Logan Wright: Example (sanitized) we can ship:
```json
{"ts":"2025-05-xxT12:34:56.789Z","level":"INFO","service":"runtime","component":"router","request_id":"req_01H...","trace_id":"4bf92f...","tenant_id_hash":"hmac256:9c1d...","route_id":"route_7b1...","model_id":"mdl_2f9...","status_code":200,"latency_ms":183,"input_tokens":512,"output_tokens":128,"total_tokens":640,"payload_bytes":8421,"request_shape":"chat.completions","kv_cache_hit":true}
```
No prompt/completion.

Naomi Feldman: Looks good. One more: error logs. Sometimes stack traces include env vars / config values. Are we filtering those?

Omar El-Sayed: Stack traces can contain URLs with credentials (rare but possible). We can add a regex scrubber to the collector (Vector/Fluent Bit) for common patterns like `user:pass@host` and `Bearer <token>`. But best is app-side: ensure config printers never include secret values.

Kevin Osei: Add explicit “do not log config dumps” guidance in runbooks. Also require redaction for:
- KMS key material identifiers? (those can be sensitive but usually ok)
- customer domain names? (borderline)
I think focus on secrets and PII.

Marcus Lin: Decision summary so far (please correct):
- No request payload/prompt/completion/tool args logged by default (and not part of supported min-pack troubleshooting)
- Headers never logged; explicit denylist + regex redact
- Strip query params from URLs
- `tenant_id` -> per-install HMAC hash; no raw user identifiers; no emails
- Default omit IP; optional truncated IP behind config
- Prefer `route_id` / `model_id` over names; names optional
- Retention defaults: 7 days operational logs (customer-controlled), audit logs separate with separate guidance

Naomi Feldman: Yup. Please add to doc: “If customer enables extended debug logging, they are responsible for validating content/retention. Redwood Support will advise reverting to defaults after incident.”

Diego Santos: Action items:
- Omar: update runtime + gateway logging defaults (route_id/model_id, drop route/model names)
- Logan: update collector config with redaction rules + URL query stripping
- Marcus/Naomi: I’ll draft the policy text for the Private min-pack docs and link it in the security review checklist

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: I’ll file a follow-up ticket for a quick validation test in the smoke harness: send a request with `Authorization: Bearer abc...` + query param `token=123` and assert it never shows up in logs.

Nadia Rahman: Great idea. Also test we don’t log any request body when a 500 is triggered.

Kevin Osei: Please loop me in on the retention wording. Some customers will ask “what does Redwood recommend” and we should be consistent.

Marcus Lin: Will do. Marking this thread resolved once the doc PR is up + redaction test is in CI.

source.text:
Naomi Feldman: Dropping the latest draft of the regulated workloads Security FAQ here for claims review. This is the version Sales will send + we’ll link from the landing page. Please flag any risky language + what evidence we need to attach/link. (Drive export -> Confluence page later today)

Soojin Lee: thx @Naomi Feldman — key deltas since last pass: added SSO/SAML section, tightened retention language, added “audit logs” bullets. Still seeing some pushback from Marketing on removing “HIPAA-ready” so we need a clear recommendation.

Olga Petrov: I’m reading through now. Starting a running list of “approved vs don’t say” lines.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: +1, let’s keep this conservative. Regulated buyers will scrutinize every adjective.

Daniel Carter: If you call out evidence needed, I can map to what’s already in the evidence pack index (SOC2 request process, diagrams, sample logs) and add missing artifacts.

Naomi Feldman: Great. Please reply in-thread with exact line edits if possible.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Compliance posture”
Current line: “Redwood is SOC 2 certified and ISO 27001 compliant.”
- Change to: “Redwood maintains a SOC 2 Type II report (available under NDA). Redwood operates an ISO 27001-aligned security program.”
Reason: we shouldn’t say “ISO certified” unless we have the actual certificate + scope handy.
Evidence link needed: SOC2 request instructions + (if applicable) ISO cert + statement of applicability.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Agree. Also add scope qualifier.
Suggested add: “SOC 2 report covers Redwood Inference’s hosted service and corporate systems (details in report).”
Don’t imply it covers on-prem customer environments.

Naomi Feldman: Noted. We do have SOC2 Type II; ISO is “aligned” not certified today.

Soojin Lee: Copy question: can we still say “SOC2 Type II” explicitly? (Marketing wants it in the first 2 paragraphs.)

Olga Petrov: Yes, “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” is fine.

Elliot Price: From docs perspective, I’ll need a stable URL to point to (internal evidence index page + external “request SOC2” flow). If we’re gating behind NDA, make that explicit.

Kira Thompson: +1. Also please avoid “certified” in general—Docs keeps getting called on it.

Olga Petrov: Section: “HIPAA / PHI”
Current line: “HIPAA-ready deployments for PHI workloads.”
- Recommend remove entirely. Replace with: “We support deployment patterns used by customers building PHI-adjacent workflows (e.g., VPC/private networking, encryption, access controls, audit logs). Customers are responsible for determining HIPAA applicability and executing a BAA where required.”
Reason: “HIPAA-ready” reads like a compliance claim.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Strong agree. Also do not say “HIPAA compliant.” If someone asks, the only safe answer is: we can support a BAA on Dedicated/Private subject to review.

Soojin Lee: Understood. Can we say “BAA available”? Or is that too strong?

Naomi Feldman: We can say: “BAA considered/available for Dedicated/Private with appropriate support tier” (we need Legal’s final word). Let’s avoid promising it in the general FAQ.

Ben Carter: (Marketing) ok, we’ll drop “HIPAA-ready.” We’ll use “PHI-adjacent” language + steer CTA to security review call.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Data usage / training”
Current line: “We never train on customer data.”
- Change to: “We do not use customer prompts or outputs to train Redwood models without explicit customer agreement.”
Reason: “never” + “customer data” is broad (logs/telemetry). Also if we use any data for abuse monitoring, must caveat.
Evidence: internal policy excerpt we can share + DPA language reference.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Yes, and add: “Data is processed solely to provide the service, maintain security, and meet contractual obligations.”

Naomi Feldman: Good catch. We also need to align with DPA wording.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Retention”
Current line: “Zero data retention by default.”
- This is risky. Replace with: “Configurable retention options are available. By default, Redwood retains limited service logs for a defined period for reliability and security monitoring. Retention periods can be reduced for Dedicated/Private subject to configuration and contract.”
Reason: “zero retention” invites a gotcha unless we can guarantee across all systems.
Evidence: retention policy statement + actual default durations (we need to confirm exact numbers!).

Kira Thompson: +1000. “Zero retention” also conflicts with audit/logging story.

Markus Klein: From SE side, we’ve been saying “we can do reduced retention + customer-managed log export” for Private. Agree w/ Olga. Also: if asked, we can describe “payload logging disabled” separately from “system logs.”

Naomi Feldman: Action item for me: confirm default durations + what’s actually configurable per product (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). I’ll sync with Product.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Encryption”
Current line: “All data is encrypted at rest and in transit with customer-managed keys.”
- Split into two bullets:
  1) “Data in transit is protected using TLS.”
  2) “Data at rest is encrypted. For Private deployments, customer-managed key integrations (KMS/HSM) are available.”
Reason: “customer-managed keys” is not universally true (Hosted vs Private).
Evidence: encryption diagram + KMS integration doc snippet.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also avoid saying HSM unless we can name supported integrations. Say “KMS/HSM integrations available” only if list is in evidence pack or docs.

Daniel Carter: We have an “encryption + key management” diagram in the evidence pack index (internal). I can add a short list of supported providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) and note “HSM via customer KMS” wording.

Elliot Price: Please give me exact phrasing; docs links need to match to avoid drift.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Audit logging”
Current line: “Full audit logs for all actions.”
- Change to: “Audit logs capture key administrative and access events (e.g., user/role changes, auth events, key configuration changes, model deployment actions). Audit logs can be exported to customer systems in Dedicated/Private.”
Reason: “full” and “all actions” are absolute.
Evidence: sample audit event schema + list of event types.

Naomi Feldman: Yes, and we should align to what’s in the engineering ticket. @Daniel Carter can you link the sample payload we used with Brightline?

Daniel Carter: I can. There’s a sample audit log payload we can sanitize + include as “example only.” I’ll reference it in the evidence pack index and we can link from FAQ.

Olga Petrov: Section: “SSO / SAML / SCIM”
Current line: “SAML and SCIM supported.”
- Add qualifiers: “SSO via SAML 2.0 supported for Console access (Dedicated/Private). SCIM provisioning supported for enterprise plans.”
Reason: we’ve had edge cases + plan gating.
Evidence: SSO setup guide + known limitations note (metadata validation, required attributes).

Markus Klein: +1. We have an open/closed support ticket on metadata validation. Don’t promise “any IdP works out of the box.”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Add “RBAC” detail too: “Role-based access controls available; permissions vary by role.” Keep it short.

Soojin Lee: Quick question: ok to say “supports data residency / regional isolation”? PMM wants it for EMEA.

Naomi Feldman: Only if we phrase carefully.
Suggested: “Regional deployment options available. For Private deployments, customers control the region/VPC where services run. For Hosted, region availability depends on Redwood’s cloud regions.”
Don’t say “guaranteed residency” unless contract + region specified.

Olga Petrov: +1. Avoid “data never leaves region” unless we can prove for all telemetry/support tooling.

Elliot Price: If we include residency, we need a footnote pointing to the “regional availability” page + “support access” statement.

Daniel Carter: Evidence pack currently has a short “data residency / support access” statement. I’ll verify it’s up to date.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also: remove any mention of FedRAMP / PCI unless we actually have it. I saw “federal-ready” in one earlier draft.

Soojin Lee: Confirmed: no FedRAMP/PCI language in this latest version. (We had it in comments only; I’ll delete comment threads before publishing.)

Naomi Feldman: Thank you. Summary of changes we’ll make before this goes to Sales:
- Replace “ISO compliant/certified” with “ISO-aligned program” (unless we find cert)
- Remove “HIPAA-ready” and avoid “HIPAA compliant”; add PHI-adjacent + BAA caveat
- Replace “never train” + “zero retention” absolutes with qualified policy language
- Split encryption claims + scope customer-managed keys to Private
- Replace “full audit logs for all actions” with event-based wording + export qualifier
- Add plan/scope qualifiers to SAML/SCIM
- Tighten residency language + add footnotes

Ben Carter: What’s the SLA on getting this updated? We want landing page copy lock by tomorrow noon PT.

Olga Petrov: I can turn edits in ~2 hours once Soojin shares the exact doc link (not the PDF export). Need to ensure track-changes is captured.

Soojin Lee: I’ll push an updated draft in the next 30 mins and @mention you + Naomi.

Daniel Carter: I’ll update the evidence pack index with:
- SOC2 request instructions (NDA gating)
- encryption/key management diagram + supported KMS list
- sanitized audit log sample payload
- retention policy statement (pending Naomi confirming defaults)

Elliot Price: Once the language is finalized, I’ll add cross-links in the docs PR (regulated landing page -> security FAQ -> evidence pack index). Please keep the external FAQ consistent with the internal Confluence version.

Kira Thompson: Also please add a “Last reviewed by Security” line + date at bottom of the FAQ. Helps with sales reuse.

Naomi Feldman: Good call. I’ll add “Reviewed: Security (GRC) / Legal: as needed” + review cadence.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Final note: wherever we say “available,” add “subject to plan and contract” once. Otherwise Sales will screenshot the FAQ and treat it as a promise. :)

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: will do. I’ll post the revised version for a quick final scan later today.

Olga Petrov: (reaction) :white_check_mark:

Daniel Carter: (reaction) :paperclip:

Elliot Price: (reaction) :eyes:

Markus Klein: From SE side, we’ve been saying “we can do reduced retention + customer-managed log export” for Private. Agree w/ Olga. Also: if asked, we can describe “payload logging disabled” separately from “system logs.”

Naomi Feldman: Action item for me: confirm default durations + what’s actually configurable per product (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). I’ll sync with Product.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Encryption”
Current line: “All data is encrypted at rest and in transit with customer-managed keys.”
- Split into two bullets:
  1) “Data in transit is protected using TLS.”
  2) “Data at rest is encrypted. For Private deployments, customer-managed key integrations (KMS/HSM) are available.”
Reason: “customer-managed keys” is not universally true (Hosted vs Private).
Evidence: encryption diagram + KMS integration doc snippet.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also avoid saying HSM unless we can name supported integrations. Say “KMS/HSM integrations available” only if list is in evidence pack or docs.

Daniel Carter: We have an “encryption + key management” diagram in the evidence pack index (internal). I can add a short list of supported providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) and note “HSM via customer KMS” wording.

Elliot Price: Please give me exact phrasing; docs links need to match to avoid drift.

Olga Petrov: Section: “Audit logging”
Current line: “Full audit logs for all actions.”
- Change to: “Audit logs capture key administrative and access events (e.g., user/role changes, auth events, key configuration changes, model deployment actions). Audit logs can be exported to customer systems in Dedicated/Private.”
Reason: “full” and “all actions” are absolute.
Evidence: sample audit event schema + list of event types.

Naomi Feldman: Yes, and we should align to what’s in the engineering ticket. @Daniel Carter can you link the sample payload we used with Brightline?

Daniel Carter: I can. There’s a sample audit log payload we can sanitize + include as “example only.” I’ll reference it in the evidence pack index and we can link from FAQ.

Olga Petrov: Section: “SSO / SAML / SCIM”
Current line: “SAML and SCIM supported.”
- Add qualifiers: “SSO via SAML 2.0 supported for Console access (Dedicated/Private). SCIM provisioning supported for enterprise plans.”
Reason: we’ve had edge cases + plan gating.
Evidence: SSO setup guide + known limitations note (metadata validation, required attributes).

Markus Klein: +1. We have an open/closed support ticket on metadata validation. Don’t promise “any IdP works out of the box.”

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Add “RBAC” detail too: “Role-based access controls available; permissions vary by role.” Keep it short.

Soojin Lee: Quick question: ok to say “supports data residency / regional isolation”? PMM wants it for EMEA.

Naomi Feldman: Only if we phrase carefully.
Suggested: “Regional deployment options available. For Private deployments, customers control the region/VPC where services run. For Hosted, region availability depends on Redwood’s cloud regions.”
Don’t say “guaranteed residency” unless contract + region specified.

Olga Petrov: +1. Avoid “data never leaves region” unless we can prove for all telemetry/support tooling.

Elliot Price: If we include residency, we need a footnote pointing to the “regional availability” page + “support access” statement.

Daniel Carter: Evidence pack currently has a short “data residency / support access” statement. I’ll verify it’s up to date.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also: remove any mention of FedRAMP / PCI unless we actually have it. I saw “federal-ready” in one earlier draft.

Soojin Lee: Confirmed: no FedRAMP/PCI language in this latest version. (We had it in comments only; I’ll delete comment threads before publishing.)

Naomi Feldman: Thank you. Summary of changes we’ll make before this goes to Sales:
- Replace “ISO compliant/certified” with “ISO-aligned program” (unless we find cert)
- Remove “HIPAA-ready” and avoid “HIPAA compliant”; add PHI-adjacent + BAA caveat
- Replace “never train” + “zero retention” absolutes with qualified policy language
- Split encryption claims + scope customer-managed keys to Private
- Replace “full audit logs for all actions” with event-based wording + export qualifier
- Add plan/scope qualifiers to SAML/SCIM
- Tighten residency language + add footnotes

Ben Carter: What’s the SLA on getting this updated? We want landing page copy lock by tomorrow noon PT.

Olga Petrov: I can turn edits in ~2 hours once Soojin shares the exact doc link (not the PDF export). Need to ensure track-changes is captured.

Soojin Lee: I’ll push an updated draft in the next 30 mins and @mention you + Naomi.

Daniel Carter: I’ll update the evidence pack index with:
- SOC2 request instructions (NDA gating)
- encryption/key management diagram + supported KMS list
- sanitized audit log sample payload
- retention policy statement (pending Naomi confirming defaults)

Elliot Price: Once the language is finalized, I’ll add cross-links in the docs PR (regulated landing page -> security FAQ -> evidence pack index). Please keep the external FAQ consistent with the internal Confluence version.

Kira Thompson: Also please add a “Last reviewed by Security” line + date at bottom of the FAQ. Helps with sales reuse.

Naomi Feldman: Good call. I’ll add “Reviewed: Security (GRC) / Legal: as needed” + review cadence.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Final note: wherever we say “available,” add “subject to plan and contract” once. Otherwise Sales will screenshot the FAQ and treat it as a promise. :)

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: will do. I’ll post the revised version for a quick final scan later today.

Olga Petrov: (reaction) :white_check_mark:

Daniel Carter: (reaction) :paperclip:

Elliot Price: (reaction) :eyes:

source.messages:
Kevin Osei: Kicking off error taxonomy alignment for the KMS wrapper. Goal: same categories + log fields across Hosted/Dedicated/Private so on-call + dashboards are consistent.

Kevin Osei: Proposal (canonical `kms.error_category`):
- `AccessDenied` (incl IAM/policy)
- `NotFound` (key/alias not found)
- `KeyDisabled` (disabled / pending deletion)
- `Throttling` (rate exceeded)
- `Timeout` (client deadline)
- `InvalidCiphertext` (decrypt failure / bad blob)
- `InvalidRequest` (bad params, wrong region, malformed ARN)
- `ServiceUnavailable` (5xx, network)
- `Unknown`

Kevin Osei: Retryability suggestion:
- retry: `Throttling`, `Timeout`, `ServiceUnavailable`
- don’t retry: `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `KeyDisabled`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `InvalidRequest`

Kevin Osei: Log fields (structured):
- `dep.name=kms` (per observability standard)
- `kms.provider` (aws|gcp|azure)
- `kms.operation` (Encrypt|Decrypt|GenerateDataKey|DescribeKey|…)
- `kms.error_category`
- `kms.provider_error_code` (string)
- `kms.http_status` (if present)
- `kms.retryable` (true/false)
- `kms.attempt` + `kms.retry_count`
- `kms.timeout_ms` + `kms.latency_ms`
- `trace_id` / span already handled

Kevin Osei: Open Qs:
1) split `Timeout` vs `DeadlineExceeded` vs `NetworkError`?
2) where do we put key identifier? (I’d prefer hash only)
3) cardinality concerns for metrics tags

Hanae Suzuki: +1 on the categories. For `NotFound` can we treat alias not found + key ARN not found the same? Otherwise we’ll be debating forever.

Hanae Suzuki: Also: `KeyDisabled` vs `KeyUnavailable`? AWS has “Disabled” and “PendingDeletion”. I’d map both to `KeyDisabled` w/ provider code preserved.

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Two security constraints:
- do NOT log plaintext, ciphertext, or anything derived from customer secrets
- avoid logging full key ARN in app logs; auditors usually accept “key reference” if it’s not directly usable

Marcus Lin: Hashing key IDs is fine as long as it’s stable within an env (salted per env is OK). We’ll need it for correlation during incidents without leaking sensitive identifiers.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: categories are fine but I really want `Timeout` separate from `ServiceUnavailable`.

Sean Gallagher: We see a ton of “timeouts” that are actually threadpool starvation or downstream call hung. If wrapper enforces deadlines, then `Timeout` should strictly mean “client-side deadline exceeded before response”.

Sean Gallagher: Also please don’t tag `kms.provider_error_code` as a metric label. That’s a cardinality trap. Put it in logs only.

Allison Grant: +1 to Sean. Metrics should be:
- `dep.kms.request.count` (labels: env, service, provider, operation, error_category, retryable)
- `dep.kms.request.latency_ms` histogram (same labels)
- `dep.kms.throttle.count` (optional; same labels)

Allison Grant: If we include `tenant_id` as a metric label, it has to be *coarse* (maybe `tier` / `deployment_mode`). Avoid customer identifiers.

Ethan Park: Platform question: For `InvalidCiphertext` we need to be careful. Some callers currently treat decrypt failures as “corrupt data” and raise 500. But for certain control-plane paths, we should surface a config error to operators.

Ethan Park: So wrapper should categorize, but callers decide handling. As long as category is consistent, ok.

Benji Okafor: On `InvalidRequest`: can we include “wrong region” there? We have recurring issues in Private installs where key is in `us-east-1` but runtime assumes `us-west-2`.

Benji Okafor: That’s not `NotFound` (AWS says “NotFoundException” sometimes) but it’s a misconfig. Category `InvalidRequest` seems right, with provider code telling the story.

Jordan Lee: IAM nuance: `AccessDenied` includes both “no permission” and “explicit deny” (SCP / KMS key policy). Provider code should be logged so we can tell.

Jordan Lee: Also request context is useful: principal/role name, but *only* the role name (no full ARN). We can add `auth.principal_type` + `auth.principal` (sanitized). Might be outside wrapper scope though.

Logan Wright: Re: fields. We should stick to existing telemetry conventions:
- log field prefix `dep.kms.*` is preferred over bare `kms.*`
- but for readability I’m ok with `kms.*` as long as it’s consistent across services

Logan Wright: For tracing, span name should be `dep.kms/<operation>` and attach attributes:
- `dep.name=kms`
- `kms.provider`
- `kms.operation`
- `kms.error_category`

Nadia Rahman: Agree on not using provider error code as metric label. For logs, can we standardize names for provider-specific IDs?
- AWS: `aws.request_id`
- GCP: `gcp.request_id` (if available) + `gcp.status_code`
- Azure: `azure.request_id`

Nadia Rahman: That will help support when cloud provider asks for request IDs.

Caleb Johnson: One more request: make `kms.error_category` the *only* thing dashboards/alerts depend on. Everything else should be drill-down.

Caleb Johnson: If we do that, alerts can be simple:
- high `Throttling` rate
- high `Timeout` rate
- any spike in `AccessDenied` (usually deploy/permission regression)

Tomáš Novák: For runbook consistency, can we ensure the categories line up with what on-call sees? Example: `InvalidCiphertext` should be used when decrypt returns “invalid” and never for “timeout during decrypt”.

Tomáš Novák: Also, if we have “circuit breaker open”, that’s not a KMS provider error. Do we represent that as `ServiceUnavailable` or something else?

Sean Gallagher: Good catch. Circuit breaker open should be its own category, otherwise we’ll confuse “KMS down” vs “we intentionally stopped calling it”.

Allison Grant: +1. Maybe `DependencyUnavailable`? But since this is specifically KMS wrapper taxonomy, call it `CircuitOpen`.

Kevin Osei: 👍 Adding `CircuitOpen` to canonical categories. That’s wrapper-local.

Kevin Osei: Updated list:
- `AccessDenied`
- `NotFound`
- `KeyDisabled`
- `Throttling`
- `Timeout`
- `InvalidCiphertext`
- `InvalidRequest`
- `ServiceUnavailable`
- `CircuitOpen`
- `Unknown`

Kevin Osei: Re Q1 (timeout vs deadline exceeded): we’ll define `Timeout` as “wrapper deadline exceeded” only. Other network errors -> `ServiceUnavailable`.

Kevin Osei: Re Q2 (key identifier): propose `kms.key_ref` as a stable hash (salted per env), logs only (not metrics). No full ARN. If Private needs to show human-readable, installer can print it during preflight but not runtime logs.

Marcus Lin: Works for me. Please document the hashing approach so we can answer “how do you avoid leaking key IDs” in reviews.

Benji Okafor: For wrong region: `InvalidRequest` category + provider code in logs. Also maybe add `kms.region` (current client region) to logs.

Allison Grant: `kms.region` as log attribute is fine, metric label is probably ok too (regions are bounded). But watch out for multi-region services; still safe.

Logan Wright: If we put region into metrics, keep it as `region` label (standard). Don’t invent `kms.region` label.

Jordan Lee: For `AccessDenied`, can we add a `kms.permission_hint` (string) when we can detect common missing actions? Like `kms:Decrypt`, `kms:GenerateDataKey`.

Hanae Suzuki: That’s nice but please keep it behind a safe allowlist so we don’t dump full policies. Just hint the action + key scope.

Sean Gallagher: Can we also standardize the error message text? Like `KMS decrypt failed (AccessDenied): missing kms:Decrypt` so it’s searchable.

Kevin Osei: Action items:
1) I’ll update wrapper spec + ticket to include `CircuitOpen` and the retryability matrix.
2) Logan/Nadia: confirm metric names/labels: `dep.kms.request.*` + `operation/provider/error_category/retryable/region/env/service`.
3) Hanae/Jordan: draft `AccessDenied` remediation hints (allowlisted) for operator-facing errors.

Kevin Osei: If no objections, I’ll codify this in the PR as:
- code: `KmsErrorCategory` enum
- logs: `kms.*` attributes (and standard `dep.name`)
- metrics: `dep.kms.request.count`, `dep.kms.request.latency_ms`

Allison Grant: No objections. Please paste the final list into the thread in eng-sre too so we don’t diverge.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also please ensure `Timeout` budgets are enforced; otherwise dashboards will be meaningless.

Marcus Lin: Approved from Security side. Ensure docs mention “no key material in logs” explicitly.

Kevin Osei: Cool, thanks all. I’ll post final taxonomy in the ADR draft + link back here. :thumbsup:

- any spike in `AccessDenied` (usually deploy/permission regression)

Tomáš Novák: For runbook consistency, can we ensure the categories line up with what on-call sees? Example: `InvalidCiphertext` should be used when decrypt returns “invalid” and never for “timeout during decrypt”.

Tomáš Novák: Also, if we have “circuit breaker open”, that’s not a KMS provider error. Do we represent that as `ServiceUnavailable` or something else?

Sean Gallagher: Good catch. Circuit breaker open should be its own category, otherwise we’ll confuse “KMS down” vs “we intentionally stopped calling it”.

Allison Grant: +1. Maybe `DependencyUnavailable`? But since this is specifically KMS wrapper taxonomy, call it `CircuitOpen`.

Kevin Osei: 👍 Adding `CircuitOpen` to canonical categories. That’s wrapper-local.

Kevin Osei: Updated list:
- `AccessDenied`
- `NotFound`
- `KeyDisabled`
- `Throttling`
- `Timeout`
- `InvalidCiphertext`
- `InvalidRequest`
- `ServiceUnavailable`
- `CircuitOpen`
- `Unknown`

Kevin Osei: Re Q1 (timeout vs deadline exceeded): we’ll define `Timeout` as “wrapper deadline exceeded” only. Other network errors -> `ServiceUnavailable`.

Kevin Osei: Re Q2 (key identifier): propose `kms.key_ref` as a stable hash (salted per env), logs only (not metrics). No full ARN. If Private needs to show human-readable, installer can print it during preflight but not runtime logs.

Marcus Lin: Works for me. Please document the hashing approach so we can answer “how do you avoid leaking key IDs” in reviews.

Benji Okafor: For wrong region: `InvalidRequest` category + provider code in logs. Also maybe add `kms.region` (current client region) to logs.

Allison Grant: `kms.region` as log attribute is fine, metric label is probably ok too (regions are bounded). But watch out for multi-region services; still safe.

Logan Wright: If we put region into metrics, keep it as `region` label (standard). Don’t invent `kms.region` label.

Jordan Lee: For `AccessDenied`, can we add a `kms.permission_hint` (string) when we can detect common missing actions? Like `kms:Decrypt`, `kms:GenerateDataKey`.

Hanae Suzuki: That’s nice but please keep it behind a safe allowlist so we don’t dump full policies. Just hint the action + key scope.

Sean Gallagher: Can we also standardize the error message text? Like `KMS decrypt failed (AccessDenied): missing kms:Decrypt` so it’s searchable.

Kevin Osei: Action items:
1) I’ll update wrapper spec + ticket to include `CircuitOpen` and the retryability matrix.
2) Logan/Nadia: confirm metric names/labels: `dep.kms.request.*` + `operation/provider/error_category/retryable/region/env/service`.
3) Hanae/Jordan: draft `AccessDenied` remediation hints (allowlisted) for operator-facing errors.

Kevin Osei: If no objections, I’ll codify this in the PR as:
- code: `KmsErrorCategory` enum
- logs: `kms.*` attributes (and standard `dep.name`)
- metrics: `dep.kms.request.count`, `dep.kms.request.latency_ms`

Allison Grant: No objections. Please paste the final list into the thread in eng-sre too so we don’t diverge.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also please ensure `Timeout` budgets are enforced; otherwise dashboards will be meaningless.

Marcus Lin: Approved from Security side. Ensure docs mention “no key material in logs” explicitly.

Kevin Osei: Cool, thanks all. I’ll post final taxonomy in the ADR draft + link back here. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Marcus Lin: hey folks — pulling together audit log reqs for the model metadata + changelog project. we’ve got enterprise customers asking “who changed what/when” for model version promos + metadata edits.

Need alignment on:
1) audit event types we must emit (create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback?)
2) what fields must be captured (actor, diff, etc.)
3) retention expectations (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
4) SIEM export requirements

Looping in @Naomi Feldman @Kevin Osei @Olga Petrov @Svetlana Petrenko + @Michael Nguyen @Ethan Park

Naomi Feldman: +1, this is coming up in procurement language now ("change management", "immutable audit trail"). we should define a minimum bar for GA and a higher bar for regulated/Private.

Michael Nguyen: from eng side, we’re emitting "model metadata changed" events for the changelog generator (public/internal) but those aren’t necessarily audit-grade (currently no retention guarantees, can be compacted). separate audit stream makes sense.

Kevin Osei: yeah, changelog events != audit logs. audit logs need:
- append-only storage
- strict RBAC on read
- consistent schema + versioning
- retention policy + export
- tamper evidence (at least write-once semantics, or integrity checks)

Chloe Martin: question — do we need to log *every* metadata field change, including internal-only notes? or just a summary + pointer to the new record?

Olga Petrov: from compliance POV: you want both.
- summary for humans (“updated tokenizer requirement”, “changed rollout eligibility”)
- machine fields to reconstruct exactly what changed (or at minimum the before/after hashes + record IDs)
But: internal-only fields can still be in audit logs if access is restricted. Don’t put internal fields in customer-visible release notes, but audit logs can include them.

Svetlana Petrenko: + also needs to work for Private where the customer is the operator. They’ll want the raw audit events shipped to their SIEM, and in some cases retained locally for years.

Ethan Park: current plan in ENG-9828 is to emit audit events from control plane on:
- model_version.created
- model_version.promoted (aka “made available for X deployment mode/region”)
- model_version.deprecated
- model_metadata.updated
- rollout_policy.updated (if it affects eligibility)
We can include a "diff" for metadata updates, but we need to be careful about size / PII.

Marcus Lin: can we define "diff" as a JSON Patch-like list of paths changed? (no full blobs) and include before/after hashes for the whole record.

Kevin Osei: that’s a good pattern.
Recommend:
- `target.type` (model_version | model_variant | quant_profile)
- `target.id` (stable registry ID)
- `target.display_name` (for readability)
- `actor.type` (user | service)
- `actor.id` + `actor.email` (if user)
- `request.id` (traceability)
- `source` (console | api | migration_job | release_train)
- `change.kind` (create|update|promote|deprecate|rollback)
- `change.paths` (e.g. ["public.release_notes.summary", "compat.runtime.min_version"])
- `before.hash` / `after.hash` (SHA256 over canonicalized record)
- optionally `before.version` / `after.version` (semver)
- `deployment_scope` (hosted|dedicated|private + region) when relevant

Olga Petrov: also include “approval context” when present.
Some enterprise asks: was this change reviewed/approved, by whom, and what ticket/PR.
So add:
- `change.approval.ticket` (Linear/Jira ID)
- `change.approval.pr` (repo + PR #)
- `change.approval.approved_by` (optional)
But make sure it’s nullable.

Connor O'Brien: on release eng side we can provide:
- PR number + merge SHA
- release train ID
- CI check run ID for the “release note preview” gate
We can populate those fields when changes come from git-driven workflows.

Michael Nguyen: we can attach PR/ticket metadata via headers in the control plane write request (console already has context). For migrations/backfills, we can set `source=migration_job` + job ID.

Chloe Martin: RBAC: who should be able to query these audit events?
Support is asking for it but we can’t have “all support can see internal notes” issues again.

Naomi Feldman: minimum:
- customer admins: can see audit events scoped to their org (and only “public safe” fields; internal fields should be filtered)
- redwood internal: limited roles (Security, Compliance, a small Support escalation group)
- super-admin: full raw event payload
We need a clear public/internal split in the event itself.

Kevin Osei: +1 to explicit split. propose audit event has:
- `public_payload` (safe for customer view)
- `internal_payload` (may include internal-only metadata fields)
BUT note: since we can’t do nested JSON fields in some pipelines, we may instead do `visibility=public|internal` + two event types, or two topics.

Ethan Park: we can do two topics (or tables): `audit_events_public` and `audit_events_internal` with identical schema, internal has more columns populated.

Svetlana Petrenko: for Private: customer will want both, but they are the operator so “internal” is still theirs. However Redwood may still want to *exclude* Redwood-internal notes that aren’t meant to ship (e.g. incident refs). So “customer-visible” vs “redwood-internal” might be better terminology.

Olga Petrov: careful language. I’d define:
- `customer_visible` (safe to show to customer end users)
- `operator_visible` (safe to store in customer environment / admin-only)
- `redwood_internal` (never shipped outside Redwood)
Then map based on deployment mode.

Marcus Lin: ok this is key. For Hosted/Dedicated (Redwood-operated), we can store all three but expose only customer_visible to customer RBAC. For Private (customer-operated), we should not package redwood_internal at all.

Michael Nguyen: makes sense. We already have “public vs internal” fields in the model metadata schema. maybe we align names now before it calcifies.

Naomi Feldman: yes, and we need tests to ensure redwood_internal can’t leak to console for non-admin roles (we had that incident last week).

Chloe Martin: noted. console side will only call the public audit endpoint unless user has `AuditLogAdmin`.

Kevin Osei: retention: what are we committing to?
Current baseline (other audit logs): 13 months in primary store, optional export.
Enterprise often asks 24 months+, regulated asks 7 years.

Olga Petrov: I’ve seen:
- SOC2: 1 year is typical, 13 months is fine.
- HIPAA / some financial: 6 years retention for certain logs (not always *all* audit logs, but change logs often included).
We can frame as:
- default retention: 13 months
- extended retention add-on: up to 7 years (configurable) OR “export to customer SIEM for their retention”

Svetlana Petrenko: for Private, we should say: “you control retention; Redwood recommends >= 18 months; export format supported.” But we also need an on-disk spool in case SIEM is down.

Ethan Park: we can build internal retention config in the audit service (by org + event type). but extended retention probably needs product decision.

Naomi Feldman: for MVP/GA, we should do:
- guarantee 13 months accessible via API/Console for enterprise plans
- SIEM export (webhook or S3 sink) for customers who want longer; we provide schema + signing
Then product can package “7-year retention” later.

Connor O'Brien: for export signing: we already sign webhook payloads in other areas. can reuse that.

Kevin Osei: also add integrity fields:
- `event_id` (UUID)
- `event_ts`
- `prev_event_hash` (per org stream) if we want tamper-evident chaining
Not required for MVP but nice.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok to scope chaining as “later”, but at least include stable event_id and hashes of the target records.

Redwood Audit Bot: Reminder: audit event schemas should be documented in /security-and-compliance/audit-logging and include data classification labels. Ping #security-review for schema changes.

Chloe Martin: question on “who changed what”: if a model version promotion is automated by policy engine, actor is service. do we also log the human who approved the policy?

Olga Petrov: yes if possible. store:
- `actor=policy-engine`
- `initiator` or `approval` fields referencing the last human approval on the policy/config.
Even if it’s indirect, it helps audits.

Michael Nguyen: we can attach `initiated_by` for promotions triggered by console rollout actions (canary start, expand, rollback). For purely automatic promotions (scheduled), we can attach the policy revision id + who last edited it.

Svetlana Petrenko: + in Private, upgrades happen via bundle install. need audit event “bundle applied” with list of model versions included + release notes artifact hash.

Ethan Park: that’s ENG-9829 territory but we can keep schema consistent.

Marcus Lin: summarizing decisions for this thread:
- audit logs are separate from changelog events
- emit audit events for create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback + rollout policy changes
- include actor/request/source + changed paths + before/after hashes + target IDs + deployment scope
- define visibility tiers (customer_visible/operator_visible/redwood_internal) and ensure Private never ships redwood_internal
- retention MVP: 13 months in-product + SIEM export path for longer

I’ll write this up + open follow-up in ENG-9828 + security review checklist.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: please also add a note that “diff must not include customer prompt/content” (just metadata fields). We don’t want accidental leakage.

Kevin Osei: +1 and redact secrets (tokens, KMS refs). If any field could include secrets, it should be excluded from audit payload or masked.

Marcus Lin: ack. will add explicit denylist + tests.

Talia Benson: jumping in late — can you tag me on the doc? need it for the enterprise beta checklist + to answer Northbridge’s security questionnaire.

Marcus Lin: will do. thanks all.

- extended retention add-on: up to 7 years (configurable) OR “export to customer SIEM for their retention”

Svetlana Petrenko: for Private, we should say: “you control retention; Redwood recommends >= 18 months; export format supported.” But we also need an on-disk spool in case SIEM is down.

Ethan Park: we can build internal retention config in the audit service (by org + event type). but extended retention probably needs product decision.

Naomi Feldman: for MVP/GA, we should do:
- guarantee 13 months accessible via API/Console for enterprise plans
- SIEM export (webhook or S3 sink) for customers who want longer; we provide schema + signing
Then product can package “7-year retention” later.

Connor O'Brien: for export signing: we already sign webhook payloads in other areas. can reuse that.

Kevin Osei: also add integrity fields:
- `event_id` (UUID)
- `event_ts`
- `prev_event_hash` (per org stream) if we want tamper-evident chaining
Not required for MVP but nice.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok to scope chaining as “later”, but at least include stable event_id and hashes of the target records.

Redwood Audit Bot: Reminder: audit event schemas should be documented in /security-and-compliance/audit-logging and include data classification labels. Ping #security-review for schema changes.

Chloe Martin: question on “who changed what”: if a model version promotion is automated by policy engine, actor is service. do we also log the human who approved the policy?

Olga Petrov: yes if possible. store:
- `actor=policy-engine`
- `initiator` or `approval` fields referencing the last human approval on the policy/config.
Even if it’s indirect, it helps audits.

Michael Nguyen: we can attach `initiated_by` for promotions triggered by console rollout actions (canary start, expand, rollback). For purely automatic promotions (scheduled), we can attach the policy revision id + who last edited it.

Svetlana Petrenko: + in Private, upgrades happen via bundle install. need audit event “bundle applied” with list of model versions included + release notes artifact hash.

Ethan Park: that’s ENG-9829 territory but we can keep schema consistent.

Marcus Lin: summarizing decisions for this thread:
- audit logs are separate from changelog events
- emit audit events for create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback + rollout policy changes
- include actor/request/source + changed paths + before/after hashes + target IDs + deployment scope
- define visibility tiers (customer_visible/operator_visible/redwood_internal) and ensure Private never ships redwood_internal
- retention MVP: 13 months in-product + SIEM export path for longer

I’ll write this up + open follow-up in ENG-9828 + security review checklist.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: please also add a note that “diff must not include customer prompt/content” (just metadata fields). We don’t want accidental leakage.

Kevin Osei: +1 and redact secrets (tokens, KMS refs). If any field could include secrets, it should be excluded from audit payload or masked.

Marcus Lin: ack. will add explicit denylist + tests.

Talia Benson: jumping in late — can you tag me on the doc? need it for the enterprise beta checklist + to answer Northbridge’s security questionnaire.

Marcus Lin: will do. thanks all.

source.messages:
Marcus Lin: quick q on legal hold + “break-glass” process for retention controls. We need to lock down: who can place a hold, who approves, and how we audit it end-to-end. Trying to avoid us hand-waving in security reviews.

Sofia Mendes: +1. From Legal perspective we need: (a) clear authority to place/extend/release holds, (b) a written reason/case ID, (c) audit trail that’s exportable, and (d) safeguards so engineers can’t silently hold/preserve data without approval.

Kevin Osei: on implementation side, we can add a `hold` object to policy evaluation that overrides TTL enforcement. But I need exact semantics: does hold apply per tenant, per data class, per region? and do we allow “incident hold” separate from “legal hold”?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: we should split:
- Legal hold (external/legal request)
- Incident hold (internal, time-bounded for IR/debug)
Both must be audited. Incident hold is the “break-glass” path but should have tighter TTL and after-the-fact review.

Naomi Feldman: PM POV: customers are asking “can you stop deletion while we’re in litigation / regulatory inquiry?” so yes legal hold. We should keep it simple in v1: tenant-level hold with scope = selected data classes (audit logs, request logs, traces/metrics, artifacts where applicable). Region-specific can come later.

Jordan Lee: re: evaluator: easiest precedence rule is Hold > RetentionPolicy TTL. i.e., if hold.active=true for a class, TTL jobs treat it as non-deletable (or skip). But we need a way for purge jobs to understand “skipped due to hold” vs “not yet eligible” for metrics.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE: break-glass needs guardrails. I don’t want an oncall to toggle a hold and forget it for 6 months. Strong defaults:
- incident hold auto-expires (say 7 days) unless extended
- paging/alerts when active holds exist beyond X days
- required runbook link + ticket ID

Benji Okafor: we can enforce required fields in the retention policy service: `reason`, `requested_by`, `approved_by`, `case_id`, `scope`, `expires_at` (required for incident hold). For legal hold, expires_at optional but we can require quarterly review date.

Marcus Lin: ok but who is allowed to approve? we need an approval matrix we can point to.

Sofia Mendes: proposal:
- Legal hold: approval = Legal (me or delegate) + CISO (Aisha) OR GRC lead (Olga) if Aisha is unavailable
- Incident hold: approval = Security Eng lead (Marcus) OR SRE lead (Sean). After-the-fact review by CISO within 2 business days.
Also: release of legal hold must be Legal-approved.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: works. Let’s codify “two-person rule” for legal hold. Incident hold can be single approver but must be logged and reviewed.

Kevin Osei: auditing: every hold action should generate an audit log event: `retention.hold.create`, `.extend`, `.release`, `.expire`. Include fields: actor, approval actors, timestamp, reason/case_id, scope (data classes), affected tenant(s), and “break_glass=true/false”. Exporter should include these in evidence pack.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also policy evaluator cache invalidation: hold changes should invalidate immediately. We already do invalidation on policy write; holds should be same codepath.

Sean Gallagher: and metrics:
- `purge_skipped_rows_total{store,reason="legal_hold"}`
- `purge_skipped_rows_total{store,reason="incident_hold"}`
- `active_holds{type,tenant}` (careful cardinality; maybe tenant only in control plane, not Prom)
At minimum we need a dashboard showing active holds count by type + age.

Naomi Feldman: UX / Console: we’ll need a scary warning modal + copy that this overrides deletion/retention, and “Export hold history” button for auditors.

Marcus Lin: also need to answer “can engineers do this without Legal knowing?” -> no, because:
1) RBAC restricts hold creation to `security_admin` role
2) legal hold requires approval fields & dual-approval (enforced)
3) every action emits audit event + change history entry
4) we notify a distribution list on create/release

Benji Okafor: notification is doable: send email/slack webhook to `#eng-security` + `#grc` (or equivalent) on legal hold create/release. For incident holds, notify `#eng-sre` too.

Sofia Mendes: please ensure the text says “suspends deletion” not “guarantees preservation” (we can’t promise beyond system limits; also backups). But for our retention controls, it should prevent scheduled TTL purge from deleting eligible rows.

Kevin Osei: good call. We should be explicit: hold applies to primary stores under retention enforcement. Backups have separate lifecycle (we can document).

Dr. Aisha Rahman: action items:
- Marcus/Benji: draft the approval matrix + semantics into the Legal Hold doc
- Kevin: add audit event fields + exporter template updates
- Sean: propose alerting + runbook steps
We need this ready for the security FAQ wording.

Marcus Lin: ack. I’ll write up a one-pager and drop in Confluence today. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: pls include “break-glass” steps:
1) create incident hold with expiry
2) link incident/ticket
3) verify purge jobs show “skipped due to hold”
4) release when done; if not, extend w/ justification
We’ll add to oncall runbook.

Sofia Mendes: and add “quarterly legal hold review” reminder. Also: holds should be discoverable in audit export (time-windowed) for customers.

Kevin Osei: makes sense. Will add those events to the standard audit exporter report template. :white_check_mark:

source.messages:
Marcus Lin: hey folks — quick alignment needed w/ GRC on SOC2 evidence for RBAC v2 work. what artifacts do you need from us (design docs, tests, logs, change records)? trying to avoid scramble in April.

Olga Petrov: +1 thanks for pulling this together. For SOC 2 we need evidence both that (a) controls exist by design and (b) they’re operating effectively over the audit period. For RBAC v2 specifically it maps primarily to logical access + change mgmt + logging/monitoring.

Daniel Carter: If it helps, auditors usually ask in 3 buckets:
1) Design: policies/standards + system description
2) Implementation: screenshots / configs showing it’s actually in place
3) Operating effectiveness: samples over time (tickets, PRs, logs, access reviews)

Marcus Lin: ok. can you be more specific for RBAC v2? like exact docs / sample types.

Olga Petrov: yep. Here’s the “minimum set” we should plan for:
- RBAC v2 design overview (entities, scopes, eval flow, deny-by-default, break-glass)
- Permission inventory / classification (what actions are sensitive, tiering)
- ADRs for permission naming/versioning + enforcement location (gateway vs service)
- Authorization standards doc (required annotations, error semantics, testing expectations)
- Rollout plan + migration/backwards compatibility plan
- Break-glass procedure doc + alerting/post-review process

Olga Petrov: Operating evidence examples (we’ll need 3–5 samples each, spread across the audit window):
- Change management: PRs showing authz changes reviewed/approved + linked ticket (Linear/Jira) + CI passing
- Access provisioning: role binding changes for users/admins (ticket request/approval + change record)
- Monitoring: dashboards/alerts for authz denies/error spikes, plus at least one alert firing + response
- Audit logging: sample audit log events showing authz decision context + export pipeline to SIEM

Daniel Carter: Also: don’t underestimate “traceability.” Auditors love when every change has: ticket -> PR -> deploy -> log sample.

Marcus Lin: makes sense. For “design docs” do they need to be frozen / versioned?

Olga Petrov: Not “frozen” but we should show version history and approvals. Best pattern:
- Confluence pages with last updated + owner + link to ADRs
- ADRs in decision-records repo with merge history
- Policy bundle releases versioned (artifact versions)

Kevin Osei: for audit logs: do we need to provide raw logs or can we provide screenshots/redacted samples?

Olga Petrov: Typically we can provide redacted samples, but auditors may request read-only access to the logging system or exports (depends on firm). We should prepare:
- schema doc (fields, meaning)
- 5–10 example events (allow + deny)
- evidence that logs are immutable-ish / protected (access controls to logging pipeline)
- retention settings + change history

Allison Grant: I can help w/ “log pipeline access controls + retention.” We’ve got internal runbooks + terraform configs for who can access the log store / SIEM exporter.

Marcus Lin: for tests: are unit tests enough? we’re adding integration coverage for critical endpoints.

Daniel Carter: Unit tests help show “built correctly,” but operating effectiveness usually wants more:
- CI gates preventing unannotated sensitive endpoints
- integration/e2e tests for key workflows
- evidence that tests run on every merge + on release branches
- sometimes a sample of a failed build that prevented a risky change (nice-to-have)

Olga Petrov: +1. For SOC2, the narrative is: “we prevent unauthorized access via enforced checks + we detect issues via logging/monitoring.” So:
- test coverage report (or at least list of critical flows covered)
- CI policy/lint output proving permissions are unique + naming compliant
- release checklist/runbook used during rollout (shows control discipline)

Logan Wright: do we need anything around cache TTL/invalidation? we’ve got metrics on evaluator cache hit rate + staleness guardrails.

Olga Petrov: Only insofar as it could undermine authorization correctness. A short section in design doc + evidence of monitoring (metrics) is good. If you have an alert when policy version mismatch/stale cache detected, that’s great evidence.

Fiona McCarthy: break-glass evidence: we need to show (1) request/approval (2) time-bounded elevation (3) logs (4) review. If we can get one real internal sample (redacted) during the period, auditors love that.

Marcus Lin: we do have an internal ticket from last month where IT needed emergency org admin to unblock a customer issue. can we use that?

Daniel Carter: Yes, as long as it demonstrates the process (approval chain, duration, and post-review). Redact customer identifiers.

Kira Thompson: I can update the rollout runbook to explicitly include “capture evidence links” section (PR links, dashboards screenshots, change tickets). Makes it repeatable.

Marcus Lin: ok. can we list the exact “evidence pack” checklist? I’ll assign owners.

Olga Petrov: Proposed RBAC v2 evidence pack checklist:
1) Governance / Policies
- Access control policy reference (company-level)
- RBAC v2 design overview page (owner + approval)
- Permission naming/versioning ADR
- Authz enforcement standard (gateway/service + annotation requirement)
- Break-glass procedure + alerting policy

2) System Config / Implementation
- Permission catalog export (current version) + role templates (YAML) + release version
- Screenshot/exports showing role bindings UI/API (who can assign roles)
- Config showing deny-by-default middleware enabled + flag status per env
- Audit log schema showing authz fields present

3) Operating Effectiveness (samples)
- 3–5 PRs: adding permissions / adding checks / changing role templates (include reviews + CI)
- 3–5 tickets: access changes / role binding requests / permission denied triage
- 3–5 audit log event samples: allow + deny + export event to SIEM
- 1 example: monitoring alert on deny spike or authz error + incident/triage notes
- 1 example: break-glass request end-to-end

Olga Petrov: Owners suggestion:
- Security Eng (Marcus): design docs + break-glass + change mgmt samples
- Platform (Allison/Logan): logging pipeline controls + monitoring dashboards
- Audit export (Kevin): SIEM mapping + sample export payload
- Compliance (Daniel): packaging + redaction + mapping to controls

Marcus Lin: perfect. what’s the “format” auditors want? single doc? folder?

Daniel Carter: We usually do a single index page + a Drive folder with subfolders:
- 00-index (mapping to SOC2 controls)
- 01-design
- 02-config-screenshots
- 03-change-management-samples
- 04-logging-monitoring
- 05-break-glass
Every sample gets a short cover note: date, what it shows, how it supports the control.

Kevin Osei: for log samples, I’ll generate a redacted JSON snippet set (allow/deny) + one SIEM-exported record. I’ll keep policy version + denial reason codes.

Allison Grant: I’ll pull retention settings + access control list for the log store + evidence of alert routes (PagerDuty/Splunk). Redactions TBD.

Logan Wright: I can export Grafana panels showing authz eval latency, deny rate, cache hit rate, and “stale policy decision” counter. We can snapshot weekly during rollout.

Fiona McCarthy: please make sure the break-glass procedure calls out who gets paged and what’s the escalation path if the approver is OOO.

Marcus Lin: noted. I’ll sync w/ Kira on runbook update.

Olga Petrov: last ask: please keep linking things back to the permission inventory. Auditors sometimes ask “how do you know you covered all sensitive actions?” The inventory + endpoint coverage report + CI gate is the best story.

Marcus Lin: got it. I’ll post a tracking doc + assign owners today.

Daniel Carter: when you have the tracking doc, add me + Olga. We’ll mark which items are “must have” vs “nice to have” based on the auditor’s prior asks.

Marcus Lin: will do. thanks all.
