Marcus Lin: for tests: are unit tests enough? we’re adding integration coverage for critical endpoints.

Daniel Carter: Unit tests help show “built correctly,” but operating effectiveness usually wants more:
- CI gates preventing unannotated sensitive endpoints
- integration/e2e tests for key workflows
- evidence that tests run on every merge + on release branches
- sometimes a sample of a failed build that prevented a risky change (nice-to-have)

Olga Petrov: +1. For SOC2, the narrative is: “we prevent unauthorized access via enforced checks + we detect issues via logging/monitoring.” So:
- test coverage report (or at least list of critical flows covered)
- CI policy/lint output proving permissions are unique + naming compliant
- release checklist/runbook used during rollout (shows control discipline)

Logan Wright: do we need anything around cache TTL/invalidation? we’ve got metrics on evaluator cache hit rate + staleness guardrails.

Olga Petrov: Only insofar as it could undermine authorization correctness. A short section in design doc + evidence of monitoring (metrics) is good. If you have an alert when policy version mismatch/stale cache detected, that’s great evidence.

Fiona McCarthy: break-glass evidence: we need to show (1) request/approval (2) time-bounded elevation (3) logs (4) review. If we can get one real internal sample (redacted) during the period, auditors love that.

Marcus Lin: we do have an internal ticket from last month where IT needed emergency org admin to unblock a customer issue. can we use that?

Daniel Carter: Yes, as long as it demonstrates the process (approval chain, duration, and post-review). Redact customer identifiers.

Kira Thompson: I can update the rollout runbook to explicitly include “capture evidence links” section (PR links, dashboards screenshots, change tickets). Makes it repeatable.

Marcus Lin: ok. can we list the exact “evidence pack” checklist? I’ll assign owners.

Olga Petrov: Proposed RBAC v2 evidence pack checklist:
1) Governance / Policies
- Access control policy reference (company-level)
- RBAC v2 design overview page (owner + approval)
- Permission naming/versioning ADR
- Authz enforcement standard (gateway/service + annotation requirement)
- Break-glass procedure + alerting policy

2) System Config / Implementation
- Permission catalog export (current version) + role templates (YAML) + release version
- Screenshot/exports showing role bindings UI/API (who can assign roles)
- Config showing deny-by-default middleware enabled + flag status per env
- Audit log schema showing authz fields present

3) Operating Effectiveness (samples)
- 3–5 PRs: adding permissions / adding checks / changing role templates (include reviews + CI)
- 3–5 tickets: access changes / role binding requests / permission denied triage
- 3–5 audit log event samples: allow + deny + export event to SIEM
- 1 example: monitoring alert on deny spike or authz error + incident/triage notes
- 1 example: break-glass request end-to-end

Olga Petrov: Owners suggestion:
- Security Eng (Marcus): design docs + break-glass + change mgmt samples
- Platform (Allison/Logan): logging pipeline controls + monitoring dashboards
- Audit export (Kevin): SIEM mapping + sample export payload
- Compliance (Daniel): packaging + redaction + mapping to controls

Marcus Lin: perfect. what’s the “format” auditors want? single doc? folder?

Daniel Carter: We usually do a single index page + a Drive folder with subfolders:
- 00-index (mapping to SOC2 controls)
- 01-design
- 02-config-screenshots
- 03-change-management-samples
- 04-logging-monitoring
- 05-break-glass
Every sample gets a short cover note: date, what it shows, how it supports the control.

Kevin Osei: for log samples, I’ll generate a redacted JSON snippet set (allow/deny) + one SIEM-exported record. I’ll keep policy version + denial reason codes.

Allison Grant: I’ll pull retention settings + access control list for the log store + evidence of alert routes (PagerDuty/Splunk). Redactions TBD.

Logan Wright: I can export Grafana panels showing authz eval latency, deny rate, cache hit rate, and “stale policy decision” counter. We can snapshot weekly during rollout.

Fiona McCarthy: please make sure the break-glass procedure calls out who gets paged and what’s the escalation path if the approver is OOO.

Marcus Lin: noted. I’ll sync w/ Kira on runbook update.

Olga Petrov: last ask: please keep linking things back to the permission inventory. Auditors sometimes ask “how do you know you covered all sensitive actions?” The inventory + endpoint coverage report + CI gate is the best story.

Marcus Lin: got it. I’ll post a tracking doc + assign owners today.

Daniel Carter: when you have the tracking doc, add me + Olga. We’ll mark which items are “must have” vs “nice to have” based on the auditor’s prior asks.

Marcus Lin: will do. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: hey folks — quick alignment needed w/ GRC on SOC2 evidence for RBAC v2 work. what artifacts do you need from us (design docs, tests, logs, change records)? trying to avoid scramble in April.

Olga Petrov: +1 thanks for pulling this together. For SOC 2 we need evidence both that (a) controls exist by design and (b) they’re operating effectively over the audit period. For RBAC v2 specifically it maps primarily to logical access + change mgmt + logging/monitoring.

Daniel Carter: If it helps, auditors usually ask in 3 buckets:
1) Design: policies/standards + system description
2) Implementation: screenshots / configs showing it’s actually in place
3) Operating effectiveness: samples over time (tickets, PRs, logs, access reviews)

Marcus Lin: ok. can you be more specific for RBAC v2? like exact docs / sample types.

Olga Petrov: yep. Here’s the “minimum set” we should plan for:
- RBAC v2 design overview (entities, scopes, eval flow, deny-by-default, break-glass)
- Permission inventory / classification (what actions are sensitive, tiering)
- ADRs for permission naming/versioning + enforcement location (gateway vs service)
- Authorization standards doc (required annotations, error semantics, testing expectations)
- Rollout plan + migration/backwards compatibility plan
- Break-glass procedure doc + alerting/post-review process

Olga Petrov: Operating evidence examples (we’ll need 3–5 samples each, spread across the audit window):
- Change management: PRs showing authz changes reviewed/approved + linked ticket (Linear/Jira) + CI passing
- Access provisioning: role binding changes for users/admins (ticket request/approval + change record)
- Monitoring: dashboards/alerts for authz denies/error spikes, plus at least one alert firing + response
- Audit logging: sample audit log events showing authz decision context + export pipeline to SIEM

Daniel Carter: Also: don’t underestimate “traceability.” Auditors love when every change has: ticket -> PR -> deploy -> log sample.

Marcus Lin: makes sense. For “design docs” do they need to be frozen / versioned?

Olga Petrov: Not “frozen” but we should show version history and approvals. Best pattern:
- Confluence pages with last updated + owner + link to ADRs
- ADRs in decision-records repo with merge history
- Policy bundle releases versioned (artifact versions)

Kevin Osei: for audit logs: do we need to provide raw logs or can we provide screenshots/redacted samples?

Olga Petrov: Typically we can provide redacted samples, but auditors may request read-only access to the logging system or exports (depends on firm). We should prepare:
- schema doc (fields, meaning)
- 5–10 example events (allow + deny)
- evidence that logs are immutable-ish / protected (access controls to logging pipeline)
- retention settings + change history

Allison Grant: I can help w/ “log pipeline access controls + retention.” We’ve got internal runbooks + terraform configs for who can access the log store / SIEM exporter.

source.messages:
Marcus Lin: Need quick alignment on terminology for the retention controls docs + policy schema. We keep mixing “audit logs” and “request logs” in PRDs + tickets.

Marcus Lin: Specifically: what are the data classes we’re committing to in v1 retention enforcement, and what do we call them so it survives security reviews?

Naomi Feldman: +1. I’m seeing “inference logs”, “API logs”, “request/response logs” used interchangeably. For enterprise questionnaires they ask “Do you retain customer prompts/completions” and “Do you have audit logs”. Those are NOT the same thing.

Kevin Osei: From implementation POV (ENG-18420/33/34): we have two concrete stores in v1:
- request log store (per request metadata, status, latency, token counts, etc; optional payload logging)
- audit log store (control plane events, authz/admin actions)

Kevin Osei: Everything else (traces/metrics) is in observability stack w/ its own retention config + knobs depending on Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Olga Petrov: Please don’t call request logs “audit logs” anywhere. Customers interpret “audit log” as immutable security/admin trail (who changed settings, who accessed what, etc). SOC2 language gets very specific.

Olga Petrov: Suggest:
- “Audit Logs (Security Events)” = admin/control plane events
- “Request/Response Logs (Inference Telemetry)” = API request metadata + optionally prompt/completion content if enabled

Jordan Lee: For policy schema, I’d rather use data-class IDs that are explicit and stable. Something like:
- audit_logs
- request_response_logs
- traces
- metrics
- artifacts (eval datasets, etc)

Jordan Lee: But we can say only audit_logs + request_response_logs are enforced by Redwood-managed purge jobs in v1.

Ethan Park: Agree. Also note: “prompt/completion content” should be described as *payload logging* as a sub-setting, not a separate data class (otherwise folks assume we always store it somewhere).

Naomi Feldman: Yeah, important nuance:
- We always have operational metadata (request id, model, latency, status, token counts)
- We MAY store prompts/completions depending on logging mode + customer settings

Naomi Feldman: Can we say “request/response logs may include customer content (prompt/completion) if configured; otherwise metadata-only”?

Sofia Mendes: From legal/compliance wording: avoid “customer content” without qualifiers. Use “prompt/completion payloads” and specify it’s optional and customer-configurable.

Sofia Mendes: Also define “retention period” vs “purge” vs “deletion SLA” carefully. If we say “immediate delete” anywhere, that becomes a promise.

Marcus Lin: Ok so what’s in-scope for v1 enforcement and what’s just “reported” / “best effort”?

Kevin Osei: My recommendation:
**In-scope enforcement v1**
1) audit_logs: TTL + purge job + exporter windows
2) request_response_logs: TTL + purge job

Kevin Osei: **Explicitly out-of-scope for v1 enforcement** (but we can include in docs as “separately configured”):
- traces/metrics retention (depends on observability backend + deployment)
- artifacts like eval datasets
- caches (KV/prefix cache) — ephemeral by design, not “retention controls”

Olga Petrov: +1. For the compliance pack baseline, we can still include a “Coverage Summary” section that lists all data classes and flags enforcement status:
- Enforced by retention policy service (v1)
- Configurable via platform defaults / external system (deployment-dependent)
- Not stored by Redwood (or ephemeral)

Olga Petrov: That makes it defensible when auditors ask “what about metrics/traces”.

Jordan Lee: One more: naming “request logs” vs “inference logs”. “Inference logs” reads like model internals / weights (scary). “Request/Response Logs” is clearer.

Ethan Park: Also “API Logs” can be confused with API gateway/access logs (ELB / Nginx). We should reserve “access logs” for edge/proxy logs.

Marcus Lin: Yes, good call. So:
- Access logs = edge/proxy
- Request/Response logs = application inference telemetry
- Audit logs = admin/security events

Naomi Feldman: Can we add a line in the docs that audit logs “do not include prompts/completions”? That question comes up *constantly*.

Kevin Osei: Yup. Audit log events are like: policy change, key rotation, role assignment, login events, report export generated, etc. No prompt content.

Sofia Mendes: Please make sure we also don’t imply audit logs are immutable/WORM unless we actually provide that. We can say “append-only with restricted access” and “tamper-evident controls” if accurate, but not “immutable”.

Olga Petrov: +1. For SOC2 narratives we typically say: “Audit logs are generated for administrative actions and retained per configured retention period. Access is restricted and changes are logged.”

Priya Natarajan: From engineering scope: keep v1 tight. If we expand to traces/metrics enforcement now, it turns into 3 different backends across Hosted vs Private.

Priya Natarajan: I’m ok with “coverage summary” in report pack, but enforcement jobs should be only for the two core stores this cycle.

Marcus Lin: Cool. Action items:
1) I’ll update terminology in the Confluence overview + FAQ draft to use the three buckets (access logs / request-response logs / audit logs)
2) Jordan/Ethan: update schema naming to `audit_logs` + `request_response_logs`, and add notes about payload logging being optional
3) Olga: for compliance pack, add “Coverage Summary” table w/ enforcement status + where configured for traces/metrics

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: I’ll ping Elliot/Kira once language is final so docs are consistent.

Kevin Osei: Also: we should call out “legal hold” as applying to audit_logs + request_response_logs (where stored) but *not* to ephemeral caches. Folks asked this in Northbridge call.

Marcus Lin: Good point. I’ll add a “Not applicable: caches” line so it’s explicit.

Olga Petrov: One more request: define “request/response logs” as “logs generated by Redwood inference API/service” so customers don’t assume it includes their app logs.

Marcus Lin: Yep. I’ll incorporate.

Jordan Lee: I’ll open a small PR to rename “inference_logs” -> “request_response_logs” in the evaluator module + add aliasing for backwards compat (so configs don’t break). Will loop you all in.

Ethan Park: Please make sure error messages match the customer-facing terminology (e.g., “unknown data class request_response_logs”). We should keep IDs stable but error text friendly.

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. I’ll post updated definitions here once docs PR is up.

2) Jordan/Ethan: update schema naming to `audit_logs` + `request_response_logs`, and add notes about payload logging being optional
3) Olga: for compliance pack, add “Coverage Summary” table w/ enforcement status + where configured for traces/metrics

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: I’ll ping Elliot/Kira once language is final so docs are consistent.

Kevin Osei: Also: we should call out “legal hold” as applying to audit_logs + request_response_logs (where stored) but *not* to ephemeral caches. Folks asked this in Northbridge call.

Marcus Lin: Good point. I’ll add a “Not applicable: caches” line so it’s explicit.

Olga Petrov: One more request: define “request/response logs” as “logs generated by Redwood inference API/service” so customers don’t assume it includes their app logs.

Marcus Lin: Yep. I’ll incorporate.

Jordan Lee: I’ll open a small PR to rename “inference_logs” -> “request_response_logs” in the evaluator module + add aliasing for backwards compat (so configs don’t break). Will loop you all in.

Ethan Park: Please make sure error messages match the customer-facing terminology (e.g., “unknown data class request_response_logs”). We should keep IDs stable but error text friendly.

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. I’ll post updated definitions here once docs PR is up.

source.content:
Marcus Lin: We should lock down denial reason codes this week so we stop bikeshedding per-service. Also need agreement on what’s safe to show to end users vs admins (Console + API).

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. Right now gateway is returning a mix of `PERMISSION_DENIED`, `NOT_AUTHORIZED`, and sometimes just `403 forbidden`. And the "reason" string varies.

Amara Diallo: Gateway middleware can standardize. But we need a canonical set that’s stable (so customers can build automation) and not too leaky.

Jordan Lee: From policy evaluator POV, we can always produce a structured denial with: (1) high-level category, (2) optional details (missing permission, scope mismatch, policy version), (3) internal trace.

Naomi Feldman: From PM side: customer-facing should be predictable + human-readable. But we should not reveal org/project names, existence of resources, or what permissions exist unless user is an org admin.

Logan Wright: For telemetry/audit, we also want a stable enum so dashboards can group deny spikes by reason.

Allison Grant: And logging needs to avoid PII/resource discovery. But internal audit log can contain more context than the API response.

Marcus Lin: Proposal: 2-layer model.
1) `error.code` (user-visible, stable) — small set.
2) `authz.denial_reason` (admin/debug + audit logs) — more granular.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Works. What’s the user-visible set? I think:
- `INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS`
- `SCOPE_NOT_AUTHORIZED`
- `AUTHZ_CONFIGURATION_ERROR` (only when we screw up)
- `AUTHZ_TEMPORARILY_UNAVAILABLE` (timeouts/policy fetch)

Jordan Lee: I’d keep user-visible even smaller. For end users, we can collapse scope mismatch into insufficient perms. Scope model is confusing and could leak whether a resource is in a different project/env.

Amara Diallo: +1. Gateway can always say 403 with `INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS`, and optionally add `request_id` / `trace_id`. The detailed reason can be behind a debug header or admin view.

Naomi Feldman: Agree. Console UX for non-admin: “You don’t have access to perform this action. Contact your admin.” For admin: show "Denied because: missing permission X" and link to role editor.

Marcus Lin: Ok, so user-visible `error.code` is:
- `INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS` (403)
- `AUTHZ_TEMPORARILY_UNAVAILABLE` (503)
- `AUTHZ_CONFIGURATION_ERROR` (500)
Anything else? (Not counting authn like expired token)

Jordan Lee: That’s enough. Then detailed `denial_reason_code` enum for audit/debug:
- `MISSING_PERMISSION` (required permission not present)
- `SCOPE_MISMATCH` (permission granted but binding scope doesn’t cover resource scope)
- `BINDING_NOT_FOUND` (no relevant role bindings at all)
- `ROLE_DISABLED_OR_DEPRECATED` (binding points to role not active / removed)
- `POLICY_VERSION_NOT_FOUND` (requested policy bundle version missing)
- `EVALUATOR_TIMEOUT` (policy eval exceeded budget)
- `EVALUATOR_UNAVAILABLE` (policy service down / network)
- `CACHE_STALE_RETRY` (stale cache detected, retry attempted, still failed)
- `BREAK_GLASS_REQUIRED` (explicitly blocked unless break-glass flow)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: `BREAK_GLASS_REQUIRED` is interesting. We’ve talked about some operations being "never" for normal admins (e.g., disable audit export retention?) and forcing break-glass.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Security wants a small set of actions requiring break-glass (e.g., modifying audit log export destinations for Private, disabling retention in certain tiers). If user hits those without elevation, we should return a normal 403 but in audit log it’s clear why.

Allison Grant: For audit log fields, we can store `decision=deny`, `denial_reason_code`, and for debug include `required_permissions=[...]`, `resource_scope=...`, `subject=...`. But we should be careful about including `required_permissions` in user responses.

Amara Diallo: Gateway implementation plan:
- Always send response body with only:
  - `error.code` (user-visible stable)
  - `error.message` (generic)
  - `request_id`
- Optionally include `X-Redwood-Authz-Denial-Reason: <denial_reason_code>` ONLY if request includes `X-Redwood-Debug: authz` and caller is an org admin OR has `console.admin.debug` permission.

Jordan Lee: +1 but permission name for debug may change. We can implement “debug headers only for tokens with `rbac.debug.read`” or for internal env.

Naomi Feldman: For customer-facing API, I’d prefer we do NOT require org admin; make it a separate permission so security admins can debug without being full org admin.

Marcus Lin: Let’s do separate permission: `rbac.debug.read` (or similar). That’s consistent with least privilege.

Logan Wright: Metrics wise, gateway should emit:
- `authz.denies_total{error_code=..., denial_reason_code=..., route=...}` (with denial_reason_code maybe only in internal metrics?)
We should not put denial_reason_code into customer-facing metrics.

Allison Grant: Internal metrics can include it; external customer-facing usage analytics should not.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more: how do we handle resource existence? E.g. trying to view project X you don’t have access to. Product sometimes wants 404 to avoid enumeration.

Marcus Lin: That’s an endpoint-level decision. But denial reason codes should support both. If endpoint chooses “mask as 404”, still log authz deny with `MISSING_PERMISSION` or `SCOPE_MISMATCH`.

Jordan Lee: We can add a flag from service annotation: `mask_denied_as_not_found=true`. Gateway/service returns 404 but includes internal audit event with deny reason.

Amara Diallo: Gateway can support it via annotation metadata in the route registry. But for now we should just standardize the codes; masking behavior can be incremental.

Naomi Feldman: Agree. Let’s keep standardization in scope; masking rules can be follow-up.

Marcus Lin: Decision summary (please react):
1) User-visible `error.code` set = `INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS`, `AUTHZ_TEMPORARILY_UNAVAILABLE`, `AUTHZ_CONFIGURATION_ERROR`.
2) Admin/debug/audit `denial_reason_code` enum as Jordan listed (we can tweak names), stable across services.
3) Detailed info (required permissions, policy version, scope) only in audit logs and optional debug header gated by `rbac.debug.read`.
4) Console will use debug endpoint/header for “why denied” UX if user has `rbac.debug.read`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: :thumbsup:

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: I’ll sync with the gateway PR for denial reason header + gating.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: I’ll update the evaluator to emit `denial_reason_code` + `required_permissions` list.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: I’ll update UX copy spec to match “generic vs admin detail”.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: I’ll ensure audit log schema includes `denial_reason_code` and keeps details out of default response payload.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll add metric tags + make sure we don’t leak reason codes into customer-facing dashboards.

Marcus Lin: Great. I’ll write up a short confluence snippet / ADR addendum with the exact enum strings so we don’t drift.

Priya Natarajan: seeing another "no outbound" request for Private. is this just BluePeak or are there more?
Hiro Tanaka: not just BluePeak. Orion Defense is the same  closed enclave, no proxy egress.
Ava Chen: +1  BluePeak called it "no egress". Orion avoids the term air-gap but same outcome.
Ethan Park: we should standardize the checklist + explicitly call out ocsp/crl, telemetry, image pulls.
Priya Natarajan: agreed. lets treat this as a repeatable requirement and keep the offline bundle page up to date.

Kevin Osei: We need to lock down the extra audit log fields for authz decisions before I finish the SIEM export mapping. What’s the minimal-but-sufficient set we want for allow + deny? Also: how do we represent denial reasons so (a) customers can troubleshoot and (b) we don’t leak sensitive policy structure.

Marcus Lin: +1. For SOC2 and enterprise questionnaires we need to show “who attempted what on which resource, was it allowed, and why/which policy”. Customers keep asking for “proof” of least-privilege enforcement, not just “we have RBAC”.

Allison Grant: From logging standards side, please keep it consistent across gateway + services. If we log decision fields, we should do it in the same event shape everywhere. Otherwise we’ll get a mess in downstream dashboards.

Logan Wright: What event types are we talking about? Request audit events only, or also internal control plane jobs (cron, background workers)?

Kevin Osei: Both ideally. First target is request audit events (API Gateway + Control Plane). Exporter pushes to customer SIEM (Splunk/ELK) and we need stable field names.

Jordan Lee: For the decision payload, the policy evaluator already has a standard response:
- subject (principal)
- action (permission string)
- resource (type + id)
- scope (org/project/env/cluster)
- decision allow/deny
- reason codes (one or more)
- policy version hash
- eval path metadata (cache hit, latency)
We can log a subset.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One nuance: gateway will sometimes evaluate “coarse” checks and service will evaluate the “real” check (depending on endpoint). We need a way to represent which layer made the decision.

Allison Grant: +1. We’ve been bitten by duplicate logging. Suggest:
- Always log the authorization decision at the *enforcement point*.
- If both layers enforce, log both but tag them clearly.
Like `authz.enforcer=gateway` vs `authz.enforcer=service`.

Marcus Lin: Denial reasons: we need a standard enum. Don’t want freeform strings.
Also should separate:
- internal reason detail (ok for audit logs)
- user-facing “why denied” copy (Console)

Kevin Osei: For SIEM export, customers want:
- actor identity (user vs service account)
- originating IP (if available)
- decision
- “permission” string
- target scope
- and a readable reason
But we can do readable = mapped from code.

Jordan Lee: Proposed reason codes (draft):
- `SUBJECT_NOT_AUTHENTICATED` (should be rare in audit logs since authn fails earlier)
- `SUBJECT_SUSPENDED`
- `PERMISSION_NOT_GRANTED` (generic)
- `NO_ROLE_BINDINGS_IN_SCOPE`
- `ROLE_BINDING_SCOPE_MISMATCH`
- `RESOURCE_NOT_IN_SCOPE`
- `POLICY_NOT_FOUND` (misconfig)
- `EVALUATOR_ERROR` (timeout/unavailable)
- `BREAK_GLASS_REQUIRED` (if we add that policy hook)
We can include multiple reasons but one “primary”.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please avoid `POLICY_NOT_FOUND` being exposed to customers directly (it implies internal config drift). But audit log can have it.

Allison Grant: Field naming: we should keep it flat for JSON logs. Something like:
- `authz.subject_type` (user/service_account)
- `authz.subject_id`
- `authz.subject_org_id`? (careful, redundant)
- `authz.action`
- `authz.resource_type`
- `authz.resource_id`
- `authz.scope_type` (org/project/environment/cluster)
- `authz.scope_id`
- `authz.decision` (allow/deny)
- `authz.reason_code` (primary)
- `authz.reason_codes` (list in string form? or repeated fields?)
- `authz.policy_version`
- `authz.enforcer`
- `authz.eval_latency_ms`
- `authz.cache_hit`

Logan Wright: Re lists: our pipeline handles arrays fine, but a few customers’ SIEM mappings are annoying with arrays. We can do both:
- `authz.reason_code` (single)
- `authz.reason_codes` (comma-separated string)
Not perfect but pragmatic.

Kevin Osei: Exporter currently supports both JSON and CEF-ish formats. For CEF, arrays are painful. I like `reason_code` + `reason_codes_csv`.

Marcus Lin: Also need “what role caused allow” for audits? Some customers ask “which role granted the access”.

Jordan Lee: That’s tricky. We *can* compute “explanations” but it’s extra work and could be expensive. The evaluator can optionally return `matched_bindings` and `matched_roles`. But I’d gate it behind an “admin debug” mode. For default logs, just decision + codes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 keep baseline fast. But for denied requests in Console, we should have enough to show “You need permission X at scope Y” and maybe “Ask an Org Admin to grant role template Z”.

Allison Grant: For “which role granted” I’d rather not put it in every audit event. It’s noisy and potentially sensitive. But we can include `authz.policy_version` and `authz.action` and that’s usually enough for evidence.

Olga Petrov: From GRC: minimum evidence we need for auditors is (1) permission requested, (2) subject identity, (3) decision, (4) timestamp, (5) scope/resource context, and (6) policy version / configuration state at the time. Having denial reasons is a plus.

Kevin Osei: +1. For the enterprise questionnaires, they literally ask “Do you log authorization failures? Are these logs exportable?” so we should ensure denies are logged.

Marcus Lin: Yes, log denies. But make sure we don’t log secrets/prompt content. Just the authz metadata.

Logan Wright: One more: include `request_id` and `trace_id` in the audit event so customers can correlate. We already do `request_id` in most services, but gateway is inconsistent.

Allison Grant: Please ensure the authz fields don’t conflict with existing `actor.*` / `resource.*` top-level fields in audit schema. If we already have `actor_id`, don’t duplicate as `authz.subject_id` unless necessary.

Kevin Osei: Current audit event schema has:
- `actor.type`, `actor.id`, `actor.email` (sometimes)
- `resource.type`, `resource.id`
- `operation` (string)
- `org_id`, `project_id` (sometimes)
We can add the authz-specific fields without duplicating actor/resource unless needed.

Jordan Lee: Suggest:
- `authz.action` = permission string (canonical)
- `authz.decision`
- `authz.enforcer`
- `authz.scope` = `org:{id}` / `project:{id}` / `env:{id}` / `cluster:{id}` (single string)
- `authz.policy_version`
- `authz.reason_code`
- `authz.reason_codes_csv`
- `authz.eval_latency_ms`
- `authz.cache_hit`
Then rely on existing `actor.*` and `resource.*`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For scope string format, please align with what we plan for the debug header too. I think PR-28241 uses `X-Redwood-Authz-Debug: action=..., scope=org:...`.

Kevin Osei: Right, exporter can just pass through `authz.scope` as string. For JSON exports we’re good. For CEF we can map:
- `cs1Label=authzAction cs1=<perm>`
- `cs2Label=authzDecision cs2=allow|deny`
- `cs3Label=authzScope cs3=org:...`
- `cs4Label=authzPolicyVersion cs4=<hash>`
- `cs5Label=authzReason cs5=<code>`
Plus existing actor/resource fields.

Marcus Lin: Denial reasons—do we include “missing permission X” vs “binding scope mismatch” etc. I’m ok with those. But avoid putting role names / group names in the audit log by default.

Jordan Lee: Agreed. Reason codes only. For debug-only explanation we can add an internal-only field (not exported): `authz.explain`.

Allison Grant: If we add internal-only fields, please make it explicit: `authz.explain` is behind a flag and stripped from customer export. Otherwise we’ll accidentally leak it in Private.

Kevin Osei: OK action items I’m hearing:
1) Add audit schema fields:
   - `authz.action`, `authz.decision`, `authz.enforcer`, `authz.scope`, `authz.policy_version`, `authz.reason_code`, `authz.reason_codes_csv`, `authz.eval_latency_ms`, `authz.cache_hit`
2) Standardize reason code enum (Jordan/Marcus)
3) Exporter mapping for Splunk/CEF + JSON pass-through (me)
4) Ensure denies are logged (gateway + services) and correlated via request_id/trace_id (Allison/Logan to sanity check)

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: Works for me. Jordan, can you also make sure `EVALUATOR_ERROR` is distinct from “deny”? Like decision=deny + reason=EVALUATOR_ERROR is fine, but we should make it obvious it’s a fail-closed path.

Jordan Lee: Yep. Decision stays `deny` (fail closed), reason_code indicates it was due to evaluator failure. We can add a metric tag too.

Logan Wright: I’ll check whether `authz.eval_latency_ms` should be int vs string in logs. But since we store as JSON, should be number. (Exporter can stringify.)

Allison Grant: We’re constrained by the doc generator here, but in actual implementation keep it typed. Also: please ensure `authz.policy_version` is stable across regions/builds (hash of policy bundle artifact, not git SHA of service).

Kevin Osei: Yep, should be policy bundle version/hash, not service deploy.

Olga Petrov: When you have the finalized field list + sample events, send to me/Daniel. We need sample evidence artifacts for the SOC2 folder + for customer questionnaires.

Kevin Osei: Will do. I’ll draft sample allow/deny events for:
- API key rotate denied due to missing permission
- audit log export allowed
- routing policy update denied due to scope mismatch

Marcus Lin: Great. Also consider redacting `actor.email` in exports unless customer explicitly opts in (some ask for it, some don’t). But that’s separate.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One last thing: do we want `authz.action` to be “permission string” always, even if endpoint uses multiple perms? Eg “any-of” check.

Jordan Lee: If “any-of”, log `authz.action` as the *requested* action or the *resolved* matched action? I propose:
- `authz.action` = the permission required for the endpoint (canonical)
- `authz.matched_action` only in debug mode
But if endpoint accepts any-of, `authz.action` can be the “set name” (like `control_plane.routing_policy.write`) while internally it’s a list. Keep it simple.

Kevin Osei: Ok. I’ll proceed with above and ping if exporter hits edge cases.

source.messages:
Kevin Osei: Need quick alignment for GRC / compliance questionnaires on “retention vs export” semantics for SIEM-ready audit export beta.

Kevin Osei: Specifically: when asked “how long do you retain audit logs” vs “does SIEM export retain them” and “what happens if destination is down for N days” — what’s the approved language?

Marcus Lin: +1. We’ve got people conflating export with retention. Export is a delivery mechanism, not a retention guarantee.

Olga Petrov: Yep. For SOC2/ISO evidence responses we need consistent phrasing.

Olga Petrov: Proposed structure:
1) Redwood audit log retention (source-of-truth)
2) Export delivery semantics (at-least-once, retries, dedup)
3) Customer responsibilities (SIEM retention, config, access)
4) Deletion semantics (customer copies are customer-controlled)

Kira Thompson: Do we want to cite specific numbers in Slack, or “per contract / plan” language? Some questionnaires force a number.

Kevin Osei: We should be able to give a default number for Hosted/Dedicated. What are we comfortable committing for beta?

Marcus Lin: Current internal target we’ve been using: Redwood retains audit events in the control plane for 90 days (Hosted + Dedicated). Private/on-prem is customer-controlled and out of beta scope.

Marcus Lin: Exporter can only backfill/replay within the retention window, and beta UI will cap the backfill window (I think 7 days) to avoid surprise volumes.

Olga Petrov: That matches what I’ve been answering: “90 days in-product retention; export is near-real-time streaming with ability to backfill up to configured limit; customer SIEM retention is customer-managed.”

Michael Grant: Legal/privacy nuance: be careful with “retain for 90 days” vs “available for 90 days.” Also make clear that copies delivered to customer SIEM/S3 are under customer control; Redwood can’t delete those.

Michael Grant: For deletion requests: Redwood can delete from its systems per policy, but doesn’t reach into customer destinations.

Kira Thompson: Ok, so customer-facing snippet could be:
- “Redwood retains audit events for at least 90 days (Hosted/Dedicated) for security and compliance purposes. Audit export does not extend retention; it delivers events to customer-controlled destinations where customers apply their own retention policies.”

Kevin Osei: Can we add the destination-down scenario?

Marcus Lin: Destination down:
- exporter is at-least-once
- retries with backoff
- DLQ for batches that can’t be delivered
- BUT if destination is down longer than (retention window or backfill cap), you can’t expect full recovery.

Marcus Lin: Also: duplicates are possible; use `event_id` for dedup downstream.

Olga Petrov: For questionnaires, I’d keep it simple:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery with retry/backoff. If a customer destination is unavailable, Redwood will continue retrying and will resume delivery when the destination recovers, subject to retention/backfill limits. Customers should deduplicate using the immutable `event_id`.”

Kira Thompson: We should avoid implying “guaranteed eventual delivery no matter what.”

Michael Grant: +1. Phrase as “designed for at-least-once” and “subject to configuration and availability.”

Kevin Osei: Also question that keeps coming up: does enabling export change how long we keep audit logs internally?

Olga Petrov: Answer should be explicit: No.

Olga Petrov: “Enabling SIEM export does not change Redwood’s retention policy for audit logs.”

Marcus Lin: Another common gotcha: “pause exports” / “disable destination” does not mean we queue indefinitely. We will queue/retry for a bounded period; after that, gaps require customer acknowledgment.

Kira Thompson: Do we have a name for the bounded period? (backfill cap)

Kevin Osei: For beta, we’ve been saying “backfill up to 7 days when you create/edit export; ongoing delivery resumes from last checkpoint; extended outages may require support-assisted replay (if within retention).”

Marcus Lin: That’s consistent with the ADR direction.

Michael Grant: Add privacy line? Some auditors ask what PII is in audit logs.

Marcus Lin: Good call. Keep it tight:
“Audit events are minimized and may include actor identifiers (e.g., user ID), workspace/tenant identifiers, action/resource metadata, and may include source IP / user agent when applicable. Sensitive payloads are redacted per policy.”

Olga Petrov: Action item: I’ll paste final approved wording into the security FAQ + retention vs export policy doc.

Kira Thompson: I can update the canned response template for compliance questionnaires once you drop the final paragraph.

Kevin Osei: Great. Can we lock the “approved language” here so Support/SE can copy/paste?

Olga Petrov: Draft (v1) — please react :white_check_mark: if ok:

Olga Petrov: “Redwood retains audit events in the service for at least 90 days for Hosted and Dedicated deployments. SIEM-ready audit export is a delivery mechanism and does not extend Redwood’s retention period. Exports are designed for at-least-once delivery with retries/backoff; customers should deduplicate using the immutable `event_id`. If a customer destination is unavailable, Redwood will resume delivery when it recovers, subject to retention and configured backfill limits. Customers are responsible for retention and access controls in their SIEM/S3 destinations; Redwood cannot delete copies delivered to customer-controlled systems.”

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: from Security.

Michael Grant: :white_check_mark: Add “subject to customer configuration” is implied, but this is fine.

Kevin Osei: :white_check_mark:

Kira Thompson: :white_check_mark: I’ll reuse that verbatim in the questionnaire bank.

Olga Petrov: Cool. I’ll thread this into the GRC evidence language email + update the docs tomorrow.

Olga Petrov: Draft (v1) — please react :white_check_mark: if ok:

Olga Petrov: “Redwood retains audit events in the service for at least 90 days for Hosted and Dedicated deployments. SIEM-ready audit export is a delivery mechanism and does not extend Redwood’s retention period. Exports are designed for at-least-once delivery with retries/backoff; customers should deduplicate using the immutable `event_id`. If a customer destination is unavailable, Redwood will resume delivery when it recovers, subject to retention and configured backfill limits. Customers are responsible for retention and access controls in their SIEM/S3 destinations; Redwood cannot delete copies delivered to customer-controlled systems.”

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: from Security.

Michael Grant: :white_check_mark: Add “subject to customer configuration” is implied, but this is fine.

Kevin Osei: :white_check_mark:

Kira Thompson: :white_check_mark: I’ll reuse that verbatim in the questionnaire bank.

Olga Petrov: Cool. I’ll thread this into the GRC evidence language email + update the docs tomorrow.
text:
Kevin Osei: Need quick alignment for GRC / compliance questionnaires on “retention vs export” semantics for SIEM-ready audit export beta.

Kevin Osei: Specifically: when asked “how long do you retain audit logs” vs “does SIEM export retain them” and “what happens if destination is down for N days” — what’s the approved language?

Marcus Lin: +1. We’ve got people conflating export with retention. Export is a delivery mechanism, not a retention guarantee.

Olga Petrov: Yep. For SOC2/ISO evidence responses we need consistent phrasing.

Olga Petrov: Proposed structure:
1) Redwood audit log retention (source-of-truth)
2) Export delivery semantics (at-least-once, retries, dedup)
3) Customer responsibilities (SIEM retention, config, access)
4) Deletion semantics (customer copies are customer-controlled)

Kira Thompson: Do we want to cite specific numbers in Slack, or “per contract / plan” language? Some questionnaires force a number.

Kevin Osei: We should be able to give a default number for Hosted/Dedicated. What are we comfortable committing for beta?

Marcus Lin: Current internal target we’ve been using: Redwood retains audit events in the control plane for 90 days (Hosted + Dedicated). Private/on-prem is customer-controlled and out of beta scope.

Marcus Lin: Exporter can only backfill/replay within the retention window, and beta UI will cap the backfill window (I think 7 days) to avoid surprise volumes.

Olga Petrov: That matches what I’ve been answering: “90 days in-product retention; export is near-real-time streaming with ability to backfill up to configured limit; customer SIEM retention is customer-managed.”

Michael Grant: Legal/privacy nuance: be careful with “retain for 90 days” vs “available for 90 days.” Also make clear that copies delivered to customer SIEM/S3 are under customer control; Redwood can’t delete those.

Michael Grant: For deletion requests: Redwood can delete from its systems per policy, but doesn’t reach into customer destinations.

Kira Thompson: Ok, so customer-facing snippet could be:
- “Redwood retains audit events for at least 90 days (Hosted/Dedicated) for security and compliance purposes. Audit export does not extend retention; it delivers events to customer-controlled destinations where customers apply their own retention policies.”

Kevin Osei: Can we add the destination-down scenario?

Marcus Lin: Destination down:
- exporter is at-least-once
- retries with backoff
- DLQ for batches that can’t be delivered
- BUT if destination is down longer than (retention window or backfill cap), you can’t expect full recovery.

Marcus Lin: Also: duplicates are possible; use `event_id` for dedup downstream.

Olga Petrov: For questionnaires, I’d keep it simple:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery with retry/backoff. If a customer destination is unavailable, Redwood will continue retrying and will resume delivery when the destination recovers, subject to retention/backfill limits. Customers should deduplicate using the immutable `event_id`.”

Kira Thompson: We should avoid implying “guaranteed eventual delivery no matter what.”

Michael Grant: +1. Phrase as “designed for at-least-once” and “subject to configuration and availability.”

Kevin Osei: Also question that keeps coming up: does enabling export change how long we keep audit logs internally?

Olga Petrov: Answer should be explicit: No.

Olga Petrov: “Enabling SIEM export does not change Redwood’s retention policy for audit logs.”

Marcus Lin: Another common gotcha: “pause exports” / “disable destination” does not mean we queue indefinitely. We will queue/retry for a bounded period; after that, gaps require customer acknowledgment.

Kira Thompson: Do we have a name for the bounded period? (backfill cap)

Kevin Osei: For beta, we’ve been saying “backfill up to 7 days when you create/edit export; ongoing delivery resumes from last checkpoint; extended outages may require support-assisted replay (if within retention).”

Marcus Lin: That’s consistent with the ADR direction.

Michael Grant: Add privacy line? Some auditors ask what PII is in audit logs.

Marcus Lin: Good call. Keep it tight:
“Audit events are minimized and may include actor identifiers (e.g., user ID), workspace/tenant identifiers, action/resource metadata, and may include source IP / user agent when applicable. Sensitive payloads are redacted per policy.”

Olga Petrov: Action item: I’ll paste final approved wording into the security FAQ + retention vs export policy doc.

Kira Thompson: I can update the canned response template for compliance questionnaires once you drop the final paragraph.

Kevin Osei: Great. Can we lock the “approved language” here so Support/SE can copy/paste?

Olga Petrov: Draft (v1) — please react :white_check_mark: if ok:

Olga Petrov: “Redwood retains audit events in the service for at least 90 days for Hosted and Dedicated deployments. SIEM-ready audit export is a delivery mechanism and does not extend Redwood’s retention period. Exports are designed for at-least-once delivery with retries/backoff; customers should deduplicate using the immutable `event_id`. If a customer destination is unavailable, Redwood will resume delivery when it recovers, subject to retention and configured backfill limits. Customers are responsible for retention and access controls in their SIEM/S3 destinations; Redwood cannot delete copies delivered to customer-controlled systems.”

Marcus Lin: :white_check_mark: from Security.

Michael Grant: :white_check_mark: Add “subject to customer configuration” is implied, but this is fine.

Kevin Osei: :white_check_mark:

Kira Thompson: :white_check_mark: I’ll reuse that verbatim in the questionnaire bank.

Olga Petrov: Cool. I’ll thread this into the GRC evidence language email + update the docs tomorrow.

source.body:
Marcus Lin: We need to lock down the break-glass flow for RBAC v2 this week. Goal: time-bound elevation + loud alerting + evidence. Can we align on (1) who can request, (2) who approves, (3) how we enforce TTL + auto-revoke, (4) what gets logged/alerted?

Fiona McCarthy: +1. Also: what is “loud” here? PagerDuty to SecOps? Slack channel ping? both?

David Kim: From IT side: we need a clean workflow for internal admins + on-call. Please don’t make it “edit a DB row by hand” :grimacing:

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Current proposal in design doc: create a temporary role binding (subject -> role=BreakGlassAdmin) scoped to org/project/env, with explicit `expires_at` and `reason`. Enforced by authz lib (deny if now > expires_at) + background sweeper to delete/disable expired bindings.

Jordan Lee: For v2 evaluator we can support `binding.conditions.expiresAt` pretty easily. But we should make sure “expiresAt is mandatory” for that role. Like: validator rejects break-glass bindings missing TTL.

Marcus Lin: Yep. I want the system to make it hard to do the wrong thing.

Naomi Feldman: UX question: is request done in Console or via an internal endpoint/CLI only? For v1, I’m ok with CLI + runbook if we document it, but we need consistent copy for support.

Fiona McCarthy: I strongly prefer we don’t expose “break glass” in customer-facing Console UI at all. This is internal only, behind admin tooling.

Olga Petrov: From evidence/SOC2 standpoint: need clear approvals, immutable audit trail, and post-event review. “Two-person rule” is helpful (requester != approver). Also need to show it’s time-bound and monitored.

Allison Grant: Logging: we should emit an audit event both when the binding is created and when it is *used* to authorize actions. Not just “binding created”. Otherwise hard to demonstrate it was only used for X.

Marcus Lin: Agree. I’m thinking:
- Event A: `break_glass.requested`
- Event B: `break_glass.approved` (binding created)
- Event C: `authz.decision` events already (allow/deny) -> must include `elevation=break_glass` and binding id
- Event D: `break_glass.expired` / `break_glass.revoked`

Jordan Lee: We can set a flag on the binding record like `is_break_glass=true` and thread it into authz context. Then audit exporter can map it.

David Kim: Who can request? If it’s internal only, I’d suggest “members of IT Admin Oncall group” + “SecOps oncall” + “Security Eng”. But approval should be Security Eng manager or SecOps lead.

Fiona McCarthy: For approvals: if this is used during incident response, requiring a manager might slow things down. Can we do: requester must be in on-call group, approver must be a different person also in on-call group (SecOps). And we still send an FYI to Marcus/Aisha.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with that. Two-person rule, but both are on-call eligible.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Implementation detail: we can enforce “requester != approver” if approval step is a separate API call signed by the approver identity.

Naomi Feldman: Can we standardize on a single “reason” format? Like must include ticket id: `INC-####` or `INT-#####`.

Connor O'Brien: Release/process note: we should require an incident ID for prod hosted/dedicated. For dev/staging, allow a generic reason.

Olga Petrov: Please do require a ticket/incident ID for prod. Auditors will ask.

Fiona McCarthy: Alerting: I want PD page for “break-glass approved in prod” to SecOps on-call, and a Slack notification to #eng-security and #secops. For non-prod, just Slack.

Allison Grant: +1. Also metrics: count of break-glass bindings created, active, expired. And a dashboard tile.

Jordan Lee: TTL: what duration are we comfortable with? I vote max 60 minutes, default 30.

David Kim: For some internal admin tasks, 30 min is tight, but 60 is reasonable.

Marcus Lin: Let’s do:
- default TTL = 30m
- max TTL = 60m (hard cap enforced by API)
- extension requires a new approval (no “extend” button)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s easiest to implement too. “Extend” tends to be abused.

Naomi Feldman: Scoping: can break-glass be org-wide only? I’m worried about someone requesting org-wide when they only need a single project.

Marcus Lin: Scope must be explicit and *as narrow as possible*. We can require scope fields:
- Hosted/Dedicated: org + optional project + optional env. If omitted, defaults should be DENY (i.e., must provide at least org; ideally project).
- Private: include cluster scope too (since admin ops).

Jordan Lee: We can encode that in the request schema: `scope_type` enum and validate “no wildcard scope” for break-glass unless `justification` includes “platform outage” and approver checks a box. But that may be too much for v1.

Fiona McCarthy: Let’s keep it simple: prohibit wildcards entirely for v1. If we really need wildcard, we do a manual path (and it’s exceptional).

Marcus Lin: Strongly in favor. “No wildcards” for v1.

RedwoodBot: (policy-bot) Reminder: RBAC v2 requires deny-by-default. New role bindings should be created through `authz-admin` service only. See runbook draft: https://confluence.redwood/security/break-glass (WIP)

Allison Grant: What about “break-glass used” alerting? Not just approved. If someone uses it to hit a sensitive endpoint, we should know.

Fiona McCarthy: Paging on every use could be noisy if they’re doing multiple actions. Maybe:
- page on approval (always)
- Slack notify on first *use* per binding id (dedupe)
- include a link to audit log search filtered by binding id

Kaitlyn Nguyen: We can add a dedupe key: `break_glass_binding_id`. Emitted in authz decision logs; SIEM rule can trigger “first seen” message.

Olga Petrov: Post-event review: we should require a comment or summary within 24 hours, and link to ticket. Can be a lightweight Jira subtask.

David Kim: Who owns the review task? SecOps or requester?

Marcus Lin: Requester writes summary, approver acknowledges. Fiona/Olga define minimum template.

Naomi Feldman: Also need a “break-glass denied” error message for internal tooling. If someone tries to create without TTL or without ticket id, error should be explicit.

Jordan Lee: I’ll add denial reason codes:
- `BG_MISSING_TTL`
- `BG_TTL_TOO_LONG`
- `BG_MISSING_TICKET`
- `BG_WILDCARD_SCOPE_NOT_ALLOWED`
- `BG_REQUESTER_NOT_ELIGIBLE`
- `BG_APPROVER_SAME_AS_REQUESTER`

Allison Grant: Perfect. Make sure those are logged too (audit/telemetry), even though the request failed.

Fiona McCarthy: One more: MFA requirement. Can we ensure requester/approver have recent MFA? (SSO step-up)

David Kim: For Okta we can enforce step-up on that endpoint via app policy (require MFA every time). We can document it in runbook.

Marcus Lin: Great. Action items:
- Kaitlyn/Jordan: API schema + enforcement (TTL mandatory, cap 60m, ticket id mandatory in prod, no wildcard scope)
- Fiona/Allison: PD + Slack alerts spec (approval page + first-use Slack dedupe)
- Olga/Naomi: post-event review template + evidence checklist
- David: Okta step-up policy for break-glass endpoints

Connor O'Brien: Please send me the final requirements so I can bake into rollout gate + runbook.

Marcus Lin: Will do. I’ll summarize in Confluence after this thread. :thumbsup:

Fiona McCarthy: :+1: and please add a test that the evaluator denies break-glass once expired (clock-skew safe). We’ve been burned before.

Jordan Lee: Good call. We’ll add unit tests + one integration test with short TTL in staging.

Allison Grant: Also consider clock skew: use server time only, not client-provided time.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Already planned: evaluator uses service monotonic clock + DB `expires_at` set by server.

Marcus Lin: Cool. Closing the loop once PRs are up.

Fiona McCarthy: :+1: and please add a test that the evaluator denies break-glass once expired (clock-skew safe). We’ve been burned before.

Jordan Lee: Good call. We’ll add unit tests + one integration test with short TTL in staging.

Allison Grant: Also consider clock skew: use server time only, not client-provided time.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Already planned: evaluator uses service monotonic clock + DB `expires_at` set by server.

Marcus Lin: Cool. Closing the loop once PRs are up.

source.messages:
Kevin Osei: doing a last pass on the audit log payload examples for optimization config changes. can folks sanity check field names + request id / correlation id story?

Kevin Osei: current example for `optimization_config.rollback` (trimmed):
```json
{
  "event_name": "optimization_config.rollback",
  "event_version": "1.2",
  "timestamp": "2025-02-14T19:57:11.012Z",
  "actor": {
    "type": "user",
    "id": "usr_4r1...",
    "email": "oncall@redwood.ai"
  },
  "tenant_id": "tnt_8k2...",
  "scope": {
    "model": "llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  },
  "from_version": "ocv_0193...",
  "to_version": "ocv_018f...",
  "rollback_reason": "p95 latency regression",
  "request_id": "req_01HT...",
  "trace_id": "00-4bf9...",
  "source": "console"
}
```
what do we want to change before we freeze?

Marcus Lin: first thing: can we avoid nested objects? i know the slack doc doesn’t care but the export pipeline does. we’ve had parsing issues in a few customer SIEMs.

Kevin Osei: yeah good call. for the spec doc we can show “expanded” but the actual emitted event will be flat. i can update examples to flat.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: +1 to flat for external. also: `request_id` must be present for *every* apply/rollback/promote/validate event. if action is from Console, we still need a request id (backend API call should have one).

Jordan Lee: naming: we’ve been using `event_name` + `event_version` across audit events, that’s fine.

Jordan Lee: but `timestamp` vs `event_time` — our other specs call it `event_time`.

Kevin Osei: ok, we standardize on `event_time` (RFC3339). i’ll change.

Felix Schneider: from Console side, we already generate `x-request-id` per HTTP request. for UI triggered actions we can forward it to the audit logger. also we have a `console_action_id` we can attach for click-to-audit correlation.

Marcus Lin: do we *need* both `request_id` and `console_action_id`? feels like we’ll confuse customers.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: request_id is required. console_action_id is optional/internal. if we include it, document as “best-effort, may be absent, not guaranteed stable”.

Kevin Osei: propose: always include `request_id`. include `ui_action_id` only when source=console and available.

Jordan Lee: +1. also add `actor_type` + `actor_id` instead of nested `actor`. better for flat schema.

Kevin Osei: ok. so fields become:
- `actor_type` (user/service)
- `actor_id`
- `actor_email` (only for user)

Marcus Lin: careful on email retention / PII. we should keep it but make sure our data classification doc covers it. for some customers, they prefer stable user id + display name.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: keep email if present, it’s already in other audit events. but not required. if absent, no problem.

Benji Okafor: on API gateway we can also attach `caller_ip` and `user_agent`. do we want those on these events?

Marcus Lin: caller_ip yes (enterprise audit asks). user_agent is nice-to-have.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: caller_ip should be `source_ip` (consistent w/ other audit logs). user_agent as `user_agent`.

Kevin Osei: got it. also question: `trace_id` vs `correlation_id`.

Jordan Lee: our tracing uses W3C traceparent; we store `trace_id`. correlation_id is more app-level.

Benji Okafor: for canary controller jobs (service actor), we might not have a trace id. but we always have a request id within control plane.

Kevin Osei: ok: include `trace_id` when present, not required. don’t add a new `correlation_id` unless we need it.

Felix Schneider: for the “diff view” and “rollback confirm modal”, we also create a `change_id` (uuid) when a rollout is started. could be useful to connect apply -> gates -> rollback.

Marcus Lin: yes please. i’d rather have a stable `change_id` than rely on joining by request ids across retries.

Kevin Osei: makes sense. how should it be named? `change_id` or `rollout_id`?

Jordan Lee: `change_id` is generic. `rollout_id` is more specific to apply/promote flows.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: use `change_id` for all lifecycle events (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback). it’s part of our internal taxonomy too.

Kevin Osei: ok, adding required field: `change_id` for apply/promote/rollback. for “create version” it can be absent (unless created via change workflow).

Benji Okafor: question on versions: we use `optimization_config_version_id` internally. examples say `from_version` / `to_version`.

Marcus Lin: prefer explicit ids: `from_optimization_config_version_id` etc. verbose but unambiguous.

Kevin Osei: agree but it’s heavy. compromise: `from_config_version_id` / `to_config_version_id`?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: keep it explicit. these events are going to SIEM. people grep fields. do the verbose thing.

Jordan Lee: +1 explicit.

Kevin Osei: ok:
- `from_optimization_config_version_id`
- `to_optimization_config_version_id`
- and for apply/promote: `optimization_config_version_id` (single)

Felix Schneider: scopes: you have nested `scope`. flatten as `model_id` / `model_name`? and `region`.

Benji Okafor: we have `model` string in most metrics. maybe `model` + `region` + `tenant_id`.

Jordan Lee: careful: we also support multi-model scopes in future. but for now can be single.

Kevin Osei: for now we’ll do:
- `scope_type` (e.g. tenant_model_region)
- `scope_tenant_id`
- `scope_model`
- `scope_region`
(keep as strings)

Marcus Lin: RBAC: need `authorization` context. at least the permission checked.

Jordan Lee: we can add `rbac_permission` (e.g. `optimization_config:rollback`) and `rbac_role` if available.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: permission is required. role is optional (roles change over time).

Kevin Osei: great. updated rollback example (flat):
```json
{
  "event_name": "optimization_config.rollback",
  "event_version": "1.3",
  "event_time": "2025-02-14T19:57:11.012Z",
  "source": "console",
  "request_id": "req_01HT...",
  "ui_action_id": "uia_9c2...",
  "trace_id": "00-4bf9...",
  "change_id": "chg_7ad...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_4r1...",
  "actor_email": "oncall@redwood.ai",
  "source_ip": "203.0.113.9",
  "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 ...",
  "rbac_permission": "optimization_config:rollback",
  "scope_type": "tenant_model_region",
  "scope_tenant_id": "tnt_8k2...",
  "scope_model": "llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  "scope_region": "us-east-1",
  "from_optimization_config_version_id": "ocv_0193...",
  "to_optimization_config_version_id": "ocv_018f...",
  "reason": "p95 latency regression"
}
```

AuditLog Bot: :white_check_mark: Schema linter: no nested objects detected. Required fields present: event_name, event_version, event_time, actor_type, actor_id, rbac_permission, request_id.

Marcus Lin: looks good. one more: can we standardize `reason` vs `rollback_reason`? i prefer `reason` across events.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: +1 `reason` everywhere.

Jordan Lee: also, make sure `request_id` is the backend request id, not a client-generated uuid.

Felix Schneider: we’ll use server-generated `x-request-id` from edge. console will display it in the activity panel too.

Benji Okafor: for service-initiated rollbacks (future auto rollback), actor_type=service and actor_id = svc_canary_controller. request_id still present.

Kevin Osei: perfect. i’ll update the doc + examples + send to Naomi for final sign-off. thanks all.

source.thread:
Hanae Suzuki: quick peer review request — can folks sanity check the example v1 audit events we’re about to publish? Main concerns: (1) no PII / no secrets, (2) stays within cardinality budget. Dropping 2 payload examples below.

Hanae Suzuki:
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01JKT4E8C0Z9T2Q9R6B8D5Z9Y2",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "event_time": "2026-02-12T18:31:12.184Z",
  "result": "success",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_9f2c...",
  "project_id": "prj_4ab1...",
  "deployment_mode": "private",
  "service": "control-plane",
  "environment": "prod",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_2e9f...",
  "actor_display": "Olga P.",
  "impersonator_actor_id": null,
  "source_ip": "203.0.113.14",
  "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (...)",
  "target_type": "role",
  "target_id": "role_admin",
  "change_summary": "permissions_added=2 permissions_removed=0",
  "request_id": "req_2d93...",
  "trace_id": "trc_8b17..."
}
```

Hanae Suzuki:
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01JKT4KZMM8ZYP7E7FWG8M1FJ9",
  "event_type": "credential.api_key.rotate",
  "event_time": "2026-02-12T18:34:09.622Z",
  "result": "success",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_9f2c...",
  "deployment_mode": "hosted",
  "service": "console-backend",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_91b2...",
  "target_type": "api_key",
  "target_id": "key_7c1a...",
  "credential_fingerprint": "sha256:7caa...",
  "request_id": "req_11aa..."
}
```

Hanae Suzuki: Questions:
- is `actor_display` acceptable? (it’s just the console display name)
- is `user_agent` too high-cardinality? we can truncate or drop
- anything else that looks like “oops that’s PII”

Marcus Lin: thx. +1 on reviewing now. First pass: `actor_display` is borderline. Display names *can* contain emails (people do weird things), also can change over time. I’d rather we treat it as optional + “best-effort” and explicitly classify as PII-adjacent.

Marcus Lin: suggestion: keep `actor_id` as the stable identifier, and if we keep a display field, use `actor_label` with guidance: max 64 chars, no emails (lint if contains `@`), and allow customers to ignore it.

Allison Grant: agree w Marcus. For cardinality: `user_agent` is a classic footgun. If we keep it, enforce truncation and maybe only keep the UA “family” (e.g., `Chrome/121` / `curl/8.x`) rather than the full string.

Allison Grant: also: make sure `change_summary` never becomes a dumping ground. “permissions_added=2” is fine; but if someone starts logging full diffs or role policy JSON, SIEM bills explode.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side: `user_agent` will blow up our dimensions if it’s used as a label anywhere. If it’s purely in the event payload (not indexed), it’s ok-ish but still adds cost + PII risk.

Logan Wright: if we want “investigation value” without full UA, recommend:
- `client_type` enum: browser|sdk|cli|service
- `client_name` small set: console-web|python-sdk|node-sdk|curl|terraform-provider
and keep raw UA only behind a feature flag / private-only config.

Kevin Osei: +1. also: `source_ip` — we should be precise: single IP string only, do NOT log full `x-forwarded-for` chain. In hosted we should log edge-extracted client IP (already what gateway does). In private, `source_ip` might be internal RFC1918; that’s fine.

Kevin Osei: On the api_key example: looks good. Confirm we never log key prefix or last4. Fingerprint only + `target_id`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: re PII: `actor_display` is where we’ll get burned. we had that near-miss where someone tried logging token prefix — same class of “convenient debugging field”. I’d prefer we drop `actor_display` from examples and move it to “optional extension field” with strict guidance.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: ensure we never emit `actor_email` anywhere. If customers need email, they can map from their IdP using actor_id.

Benji Okafor: for RBAC event: `target_id: role_admin` — is that always low-cardinality? roles might be customer-defined (role “finance-approver”, etc). still not crazy but can be many.

Benji Okafor: maybe we split:
- `target_id` = internal role id (`role_...`)
- `target_name` optional (same warnings as display)

Nadia Rahman: +1 Benji. also keep enums tight:
- `event_type` from registry only
- `result` = success|failure
- add `error_code` for failures, avoid free-form `error_message`

Nadia Rahman: for `change_summary`, can we standardize a compact structure without nested JSON? ex: `change_summary="added=perm:project.write,perm:keys.rotate removed="` might be too big, but counts-only loses investigation value.

Allison Grant: we can do both: keep a counts-only summary as required, and allow an optional `change_set_hash` or `change_set_id` that points to internal diff store (but careful: we said no new UI).

Marcus Lin: if we’re not building diff storage, simplest is:
- required: `change_summary_counts` (counts only)
- optional: `change_summary_keys` limited to small allowlist and capped length (e.g., 256 chars)

Logan Wright: strongly prefer the counts-only as required. optional list fields get abused. also for cardinality budgets, lists of permission names = high-ish.

Hanae Suzuki: ok, seems consensus:
- remove `actor_display` from “recommended” examples; keep as optional `actor_label` w lint (no `@`, max len)
- replace raw `user_agent` with `client_type` + `client_name` (raw UA optional / off by default)
- clarify `source_ip` single value only
- `target_id` should be stable internal id; optional `target_label` w same constraints

Kevin Osei: +1. Also for SIEM export, customers asked for “who did it” and “from where”. `actor_id` + `source_ip` + correlation ids are the core.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can we add explicit note in schema doc: “Do not log emails, names, prompts, or request bodies. Do not log secrets (API keys, tokens) even partially.”

Marcus Lin: yes, add to redaction checklist + lint patterns. I’ll also add a line: any field that can contain customer-provided strings must be treated as high risk for PII + cardinality.

Allison Grant: last nit: `environment: prod` ok. `service: control-plane` ok. If we add `region`, keep it as controlled enum (us-east-1 etc). Do NOT log full hostnames/pod names.

Logan Wright: reaction: :thumbsup: (added by Logan Wright)

Hanae Suzuki: awesome. I’ll update examples + add doc notes. Can someone (Marcus/Allison) do a quick final glance once I push the edits?

Marcus Lin: yep, tag me. I’ll do final pass today.

Allison Grant: same. pls include the “client_type/client_name” guidance in the logging standard too so it’s consistent.

Hanae Suzuki: will do — thanks all.

source.body:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off the naming convention thread. We need something consistent before we lock the permission registry + start annotating endpoints. Current state is a mess (some `org.admin`, some `manage_keys`, some hardcoded strings in gateway).

Marcus Lin: Proposals I’ve heard:
- `org.api_keys.rotate`
- `controlplane.api_keys.rotate`
- `redwood.org.api_keys.rotate`
- `rbac:org:api_keys:rotate` (pls no)

Marcus Lin: Main questions:
1) separator (`.` vs `:`)
2) namespacing (product/service vs domain)
3) versioning (in string vs metadata)
4) verb set (create/update/delete/manage?)

Jordan Lee: +1. From policy engine side I strongly prefer `.` separated, lowercase, deterministic segments. Easy to lint + match prefixes.

Jordan Lee: I’d vote for: `<domain>.<resource>.<action>` where domain = customer-facing conceptual area (org/project/deployment/audit/etc), NOT internal service name.

Jordan Lee: Examples:
```
org.users.invite
org.roles.update
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
audit_logs.export
```

Naomi Feldman: PM angle: agree on conceptual domain. If we namespace by service (`gateway.*`, `controlplane.*`) it’s going to leak internals into docs + customer configs.

Priya Natarajan: +1 on not coupling to services. But we do need to avoid collisions as scope expands (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). “deployment” vs “cluster” vs “environment” will matter.

Amara Diallo: Gateway needs something that can be attached as an annotation + checked quickly. Dots are fine. Please don’t do colons; it interacts weirdly with a couple of our log parsers and header formats.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For SSO/group mapping (esp Private) customers will see these strings in some capacity (Console permission viewer, docs). Readability matters.

Ethan Park: One pain point: some actions are “read” vs “list” vs “get”. Can we standardize verbs? If we have both `read` and `get` floating around it’ll explode.

Jordan Lee: Verbs: I’d propose a constrained set:
- `get` (single)
- `list`
- `create`
- `update`
- `delete`
- `rotate` (special case)
- `enable` / `disable` (special)
- `export` (audit/log export)

Jordan Lee: And avoid `manage` unless it’s truly composite. `manage` becomes a permission bucket and breaks least privilege.

Marcus Lin: Agree. We should push people away from `manage_*` unless we’re intentionally creating a coarse role template.

Naomi Feldman: What about `view` vs `read`? Some UX language says “View audit logs”. But for the permission string I’m fine with `get/list`.

Amara Diallo: We should align with API patterns: HTTP GET on `/audit-logs` maps to `audit_logs.list`, GET on `/audit-logs/{id}` maps to `audit_logs.get`. Makes it easy in codegen.

Priya Natarajan: +1. That gives us a clean mapping rule.

Brian Murphy: Jumping in — docs team will thank you if we do `get/list/create/update/delete` and avoid synonyms.

Marcus Lin: Versioning question: do we ever put `v1` in the permission string? e.g. `org.api_keys.rotate.v1`?

Jordan Lee: Please no. If permissions are part of customer contracts, changing the string is painful. Better: permission ID is stable, and we add metadata:
- `introduced_at`
- `deprecated_at`
- `replaced_by`

Jordan Lee: Then policy bundle tooling can emit warnings if you still use deprecated perms.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: If we don’t version in the string, how do we handle semantics changes? Like splitting one permission into two.

Jordan Lee: That’s a migration problem, not a naming problem. We keep old perm working (maybe mapped internally) while new perms exist. Eventually deprecate + remove with a long runway.

Naomi Feldman: I like that. For enterprise questionnaires, we can say “permissions are stable identifiers, with explicit deprecation policy”.

Priya Natarajan: +1 but we need a hard rule: never change semantics of an existing permission. If semantics change, create a new permission and deprecate the old.

Marcus Lin: That’s basically what ADR-014 should state.

Amara Diallo: From gateway perspective, having stable strings helps caching too. If we embed version in the string we’ll end up with multiple checks and weird dual-read behavior.

Ethan Park: Namespacing question: do we want `org.*` and `project.*` etc. Or `console.*`? Some console-only actions (like “view billing page”) shouldn’t be permissions at all IMO.

Naomi Feldman: Console page access should be derived from API permissions, not new console-only permissions. Otherwise we create UI drift.

Marcus Lin: +1. “Sensitive actions” should map to actual state changes / data access. UI routes should call APIs and get denied there.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For Private installer/bootstrap we might need a couple of “platform install” actions that aren’t customer API actions.

Priya Natarajan: That’s still a domain though. Maybe `platform.install.bootstrap` or `private_deployments.*`?

Jordan Lee: We can use a `platform.*` domain for operator-only actions. Keep it minimal and clearly documented as operator scope.

Marcus Lin: Ok. Another naming nit: pluralization and underscores. `api_keys` vs `apikeys` vs `api-keys`.

Jordan Lee: Use snake_case for multiword segments (`api_keys`, `audit_logs`, `routing_policies`). All lowercase. No hyphens.

Amara Diallo: +1. Hyphens are annoying in some tooling. Snake_case is easiest.

Naomi Feldman: So proposed format:
`<domain>.<resource>.<action>`
- `domain`: `org`, `project`, `deployment`, `audit_logs`, `billing`, `platform` (?)
- `resource`: snake_case
- `action`: from allowed verbs

Naomi Feldman: Small question: is `audit_logs` a domain or resource? Example `audit_logs.export` has only 2 segments.

Jordan Lee: Good catch. I’d rather keep 3 segments always. So domain `audit`, resource `logs`, action `export` -> `audit.logs.export`.

Marcus Lin: That reads weird but is consistent.

Ethan Park: Alternative: treat “audit_logs” as resource and have domain `org` or `project`? Like `org.audit_logs.export`.

Priya Natarajan: I like `org.audit_logs.export` because audit logs are scoped to org anyway (at least today). And we might have `project.audit_logs.export` later if we add project-level audit streams.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. Also makes scoping clearer in docs (“This role can export org audit logs”).

Jordan Lee: Works for me. Then domain is the scope container, not the service.

Jordan Lee: So:
```
org.audit_logs.list
org.audit_logs.export
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
deployment.model_pins.update
```

Marcus Lin: That’s pretty solid.

Amara Diallo: For routing, are we sure it’s `project.routing_policies.update` and not `project.routing.update`? “policy” is an overloaded word here.

Priya Natarajan: We should match the actual object name in the control plane: “routing policy” is the thing with fallback + region weights, so `routing_policies` is fine.

Naomi Feldman: Another question: do we include “environment” as a domain or as a scope on the resource? Like `project.environments.update` vs `environment.routing_policies.update`.

Jordan Lee: Naming doesn’t encode scope. Scope is in the evaluation input (org_id/project_id/env_id/cluster_id). Permission string should not vary based on scope.

Jordan Lee: So you can have `project.routing_policies.update` evaluated at env scope depending on request context. (Policy engine checks binding scope).

Marcus Lin: Right, scope semantics are separate.

Brian Murphy: For docs, we should include a “permission naming rules” section + a “how to request a new permission” template. Otherwise every team will invent new patterns.

Marcus Lin: Action items:
- Jordan: update ADR-014 draft with:
  - dot-separated, lowercase, snake_case segments
  - no service names in permission IDs
  - no version suffix in string; versioning via metadata + deprecation policy
  - verb set + guidance against `manage`
- Naomi: sanity-check against customer-facing terminology + console UX (permission viewer/search)
- Amara/Ethan: confirm gateway + control plane annotations can support `org.*` / `project.*` patterns cleanly

Priya Natarajan: +1. Also add lint rules in the permission registry PR so we fail CI on format violations.

Jordan Lee: On it. I’ll drop a PR comment in `pr-28134` once I’ve updated the linter rules.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: Also please reserve `internal.*` or `platform.*` now, so we don’t end up with random “ops_*” strings later.

Marcus Lin: Good call. Let’s reserve `platform.*` for operator-only actions.

Naomi Feldman: Works. One more: should we prefix everything with `redwood.`?

Jordan Lee: I don’t think so. Adds noise, no extra value. These aren’t globally unique identifiers; they’re scoped to Redwood policy bundle.

Priya Natarajan: Agree. Keep short.

Marcus Lin: Consensus seems: no `redwood.` prefix.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. In UI the shorter the better.

Marcus Lin: Cool. I’ll mark this thread as the input to ADR-014 and link it in Confluence. Thanks all.

Amara Diallo: +1. Hyphens are annoying in some tooling. Snake_case is easiest.
